About.metaTitle: About FrameTrain – Mission, Vision, Team | FrameTrain
About.metaDescription: FrameTrain was built so ML engineers and researchers can train AI models locally – no cloud dependency, no data loss, no subscription surprises. Meet the team and the mission.
About.ogDescription: Why we built FrameTrain and where it's headed.

hero.badge: Made for ML Engineers, by ML Engineers
hero.subtitle: We wanted a tool ourselves that makes local fine-tuning as easy as a single click – no cloud subscription, no privacy compromises, no hours of setup. So we built it.

origin.heading: The Story
origin.p1: The start was frustrating: we wanted to fine-tune a domain-specific language model on our own dataset. Sensitive data – nothing for the cloud. So: local training. The alternative was writing our own Python scripts, debugging dependencies, maintaining config files.
origin.p2: For someone with an ML background: doable. For everyone else: a wall of complexity. Yet the actual problem – "I want to adapt this model to my data" – is actually simple and clear.
origin.p3: FrameTrain is an attempt to make the gap between <b2>idea and first training run</b2> as small as possible. A native desktop app that wraps the entire stack – HuggingFace, PyTorch, LoRA, GPU scheduling – behind an interface you understand immediately.
origin.p4: And because we want as many people as possible to be able to use it: starting at <b3>€4.99/month</b3>. Cancel anytime, no hidden fees, no surprises.

mission.heading: Mission
mission.text: Empower every ML engineer and researcher to train AI models locally – independent of cloud infrastructure, an internet connection, or budget. Local training should become as natural as local development.

vision.heading: Vision
vision.text: A world where proprietary company data no longer has to land on someone else's servers just to use AI. Where a doctor, a lawyer, or a researcher can adapt a model to sensitive data – fully locally, fully securely.

values.heading: Our Principles
values.subtitle: These convictions guide every decision we make at FrameTrain.

items.title: Privacy first
items.description: Local training isn't a feature – it's the foundation. Your data belongs to you. We have no API that ever gets to see your training data.

items.title: Simplicity over complexity
items.description: ML training shouldn't fail at setup. FrameTrain aims to get your first training run started in under 10 minutes – no shell knowledge required.

items.title: Accessibility
items.description: The €4.99/month Early Access price is not an accident. We want students, researchers, and indie developers to have the same tools as big companies.

items.title: Open source where possible
items.description: We believe in open ecosystems. FrameTrain builds on HuggingFace, PyTorch, and the open-source ML stack – and gives back where we can.

techStack.heading: Tech Stack
techStack.intro: FrameTrain is built entirely on open-source technologies. No proprietary ML framework, no vendor-lock-in libraries. That means: when you use FrameTrain, real PyTorch runs on your machine – not some wrapper.

LoraGuide.metaTitle: LoRA Fine-Tuning Explained – Train LLMs Efficiently | FrameTrain
LoraGuide.metaDescription: What is LoRA? How does Low-Rank Adaptation work? The complete guide to LoRA vs full fine-tuning, QLoRA, and how to efficiently adapt language models to your own data.
LoraGuide.ogTitle: LoRA Fine-Tuning Explained – Train LLMs Efficiently
LoraGuide.ogDescription: The complete guide to LoRA and QLoRA for LLM fine-tuning.
LoraGuide.heroTitleLine2: Train LLMs Efficiently on Your Own Data
LoraGuide.heroSubtitle: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) makes it possible to fine-tune language models with billions of parameters on small GPUs – without retraining the entire model. This guide explains how it works and when you should use it.

whatIsLora.heading: What is LoRA?
whatIsLora.p1: LoRA stands for Low-Rank Adaptation, a research method introduced by Microsoft in 2021. Instead of updating all the billions of parameters of a large language model during fine-tuning, only small additional matrices are trained.
whatIsLora.p2: The principle: a model's weight changes can be mathematically approximated by two small matrices with low rank. These matrices have far fewer parameters than the original model – with equal or even better adaptation quality.
whatIsLora.p3: A Llama-3 model with 8 billion parameters needs about 60–80 GB VRAM for full fine-tuning. With LoRA that drops to 6–12 GB – making it feasible on consumer GPUs like the RTX 4070 or 3090.

vsFull.heading: LoRA vs Full Fine-Tuning – When to Use What?
loraItems: Low VRAM needed (6–16 GB is enough)
Multiple LoRA adapters per base model possible
Adapters are small and easy to share
Well suited for: chatbots, instruction tuning
fullItems: Better results on very large datasets
Well suited for: pre-training, massive domain language
vsFull.ruleOfThumb: Rule of thumb: For most practical use cases – chatbots, classifiers, domain-specific assistants – LoRA is the better choice. Full fine-tuning only pays off with very large, domain-specific datasets (100,000+ examples) and the corresponding GPU infrastructure.

qlora.heading: QLoRA – Even Less VRAM
qlora.p1: QLoRA (Quantized LoRA) combines LoRA with 4-bit quantization of the base model. The model is loaded in 4-bit precision, which drastically reduces memory requirements further – while the LoRA adapters are still trained in 16-bit.
qlora.p2: Result: Llama-3 8B can be trained with QLoRA on an RTX 3060 (12 GB). A 13B model needs about 14–16 GB VRAM. That makes professional LLM fine-tuning possible even without high-end hardware.

items.desc: Rank of the LoRA matrices. Higher rank = more parameters, more capacity, but also more VRAM. r=8 is enough for simple tasks, r=16–32 for more complex domain adaptation.
Scaling factor for the LoRA weights. Often set to the same value as rank. Higher alpha = more aggressive updates.
Which layers are adapted with LoRA. Query and value projections are standard. More modules = more capacity, but more memory.
Dropout rate on the LoRA layers. Helps against overfitting on small datasets.

cta.heading: LoRA Fine-Tuning Right Inside FrameTrain
cta.text: LoRA, QLoRA, rank, alpha, target modules – all configurable through an intuitive interface. No code required. Locally on your GPU.

CloudGuide.heroSubtitle: Google Colab, AWS SageMaker, Paperspace – or your own GPU? We compare cost, privacy, flexibility, and performance for typical ML workloads.

cost.heading: Cost Comparison: Cloud vs Local
cost.intro: Cloud GPUs are billed by the hour. For intensive ML projects, costs add up quickly. Local training, on the other hand, has a one-time hardware cost – no further costs after that.
cost.footnote: ¹ After the one-time hardware investment. Electricity cost approx. $0.03–0.08 per hour (RTX 4070: ~200W).

privacy.heading: Privacy & GDPR – Why Local Is Better
privacy.intro: With cloud services, your training data is uploaded to someone else's servers – in the US, Europe, or elsewhere. For many use cases that's problematic or simply not allowed:

items.title: Customer data
items.text: Under GDPR, customer data may not be transferred to US servers without explicit consent.

items.title: Medical data
items.text: Patient data is subject to special protection requirements that rule out cloud training.

items.title: Company data
items.text: Proprietary company data and trade secrets should never leave external servers.

items.title: Research data
items.text: Unpublished research results can become unintentionally exposed through a cloud upload.

whenCloud.heading: When Does Cloud Make Sense?
whenCloud.intro: Local training isn't always the best choice. Cloud makes sense for:
items: Training very large models (70B+ parameters) that need more VRAM than you have available
Horizontal scaling to hundreds of GPUs for fast experiments
No own hardware available and the training workload is one-off/low
Team workflows where multiple people need simultaneous access to the same resources

faqs.q: What is FrameTrain?
faqs.a: FrameTrain is a desktop app for local machine learning training. You can use it to fine-tune HuggingFace models with LoRA and QLoRA on your own datasets – no cloud, no dependencies, running entirely on your own hardware.

faqs.q: Who is FrameTrain for?
faqs.a: ML engineers, data scientists, researchers, and developers who want to train AI models locally and in a GDPR-compliant way. Even without deep ML knowledge you can get started with FrameTrain.

faqs.q: Which operating systems are supported?
faqs.a: FrameTrain runs on Windows 10/11, macOS 11+ (Intel & Apple Silicon), and Ubuntu 20.04+. All common Linux distributions with AppImage support work as well.

faqs.q: Is an internet connection required?
faqs.a: Only at startup for license validation via API key. Training itself runs completely offline. Models from HuggingFace are downloaded once and stored locally.

faqs.q: Can I use FrameTrain in a company?
faqs.a: Yes. FrameTrain is deliberately built so that company data never leaves your own infrastructure. There is no automatic telemetry and no cloud uploads during training – your training data, datasets, and model weights never leave your machine. You can optionally enable error reporting in the settings, which sends anonymized diagnostic data (e.g. stack trace, app version) if a crash occurs – off by default and can be disabled at any time. Ideal for GDPR-compliant ML projects.

faqs.q: Is there a free trial?
faqs.a: There is no free trial at the moment. We're planning a free tier with limited features. In Early Access, the entry price of €4.99/month is intentionally low.

faqs.q: How much does FrameTrain cost?
faqs.a: In Early Access: €4.99/month or €39.99/year (equivalent to €3.33/month). The price increases to €9.99/month after the first 100 users. Those who join now keep the lower price permanently.

faqs.q: Which payment methods are accepted?
faqs.a: All major credit and debit cards via Stripe: Visa, Mastercard, American Express, Discover. Additionally SEPA direct debit for Europe, Apple Pay, and Google Pay.

faqs.q: Can I cancel at any time?
faqs.a: Yes, at any time, effective at the end of the current billing period. No notice periods, no hidden fees. Access remains active until the end of the paid period.

faqs.q: Is there a money-back guarantee?
faqs.a: Not currently. Since this involves digital content that is made available immediately, the statutory right of withdrawal is limited. Our support team will help with any issues.

faqs.q: What happens after the first 100 users?
faqs.a: The price increases to €9.99/month for new users. Existing users keep their Early Access price permanently – even as the general price rises.

faqs.q: Are there discounts for students or non-profits?
faqs.a: Not yet, but planned. Get in touch by email – we try to find solutions on a case-by-case basis.

vramTable.intro: Required VRAM depending on the method:
vramTable.footnote: Larger batch sizes increase VRAM requirements. Gradient checkpointing can reduce the requirement.

faqs.q: What GPU do I need for FrameTrain?
faqs.a: __VRAM_TABLE__

faqs.q: Can I train without a GPU?
faqs.a: Yes, CPU training is possible but extremely slow. For productive work we recommend at least an NVIDIA GTX 1060 (6 GB VRAM) or Apple M1.

faqs.q: Does FrameTrain support Apple Silicon?
faqs.a: Yes. FrameTrain natively supports Apple M1, M2, M3, and M4 via Metal Performance Shaders (MPS). Training runs on the M-chip's GPU without requiring CUDA.

faqs.q: Which NVIDIA GPUs are supported?
faqs.a: All NVIDIA GPUs with CUDA support from the GTX 10 series (Pascal architecture) onward. Tested: GTX 1060, 1080, RTX 2080, 3060, 3090, 4070, 4080, 4090. Recommendation for 7B models: RTX 3060 (12 GB) or better.

faqs.q: Can I use multiple GPUs?
faqs.a: Multi-GPU training is on the roadmap but not yet implemented. FrameTrain currently supports one GPU per training session.

faqs.q: What is the difference between CUDA and MPS?
faqs.a: CUDA is NVIDIA's proprietary compute framework, very mature and fast. MPS (Metal Performance Shaders) is Apple's equivalent for M-chip Macs, slightly slower than CUDA but perfectly adequate for most fine-tuning tasks.

hyperparamsCode.intro: Recommended starting parameters for LoRA fine-tuning:
hyperparamsCode.footnote: These values are a good starting point. Adjust them to your dataset and GPU.

durationTable.intro: Approximate training times for 1,000 samples, 3 epochs:

faqs.q: What is LoRA and when should I use it?
faqs.a: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) is a method for fine-tuning language models with little VRAM. Instead of updating all model parameters, LoRA only trains small additional matrices. Recommended for: GPU with <24 GB VRAM, domain-specific adaptation, multiple adapters on one base model.

faqs.q: What are good starting parameters for LoRA fine-tuning?
faqs.a: __HYPERPARAMS_CODE__

faqs.q: How long does training take?
faqs.a: __DURATION_TABLE__

faqs.q: What is QLoRA and when is it better than LoRA?
faqs.a: QLoRA combines 4-bit quantization of the base model with LoRA training. This reduces VRAM requirements by about 40–60%. QLoRA is the better choice when you have limited VRAM (under 12 GB) or want to fine-tune a 13B+ model.

faqs.q: Can I use my own datasets?
faqs.a: Yes. FrameTrain supports CSV, JSON, and JSONL as dataset formats. You can also load datasets directly from the HuggingFace Datasets Hub.

faqs.q: What is the difference between full fine-tuning and LoRA?
faqs.a: Full fine-tuning updates all model parameters – needs lots of VRAM (40–80+ GB for 7B) but is more flexible. LoRA only trains small adapter matrices, needs much less VRAM (6–18 GB for 7B), and delivers equivalent results for most use cases.

faqs.q: Are my training data uploaded anywhere?
faqs.a: No. Your training data never leaves your machine. All training runs locally. Only at startup does the app check the API key against our servers – no data or model weights are transferred in that process.

faqs.q: Is FrameTrain GDPR-compliant?
faqs.a: Yes. Since no training data leaves your own infrastructure, FrameTrain is ideal for GDPR-compliant ML projects. There is no telemetry and no hidden data transfers. Payment data is processed exclusively by Stripe (PCI-DSS Level 1).

faqs.q: Are my models stored in the cloud?
faqs.a: No. All trained models and checkpoints are stored exclusively locally on your device. FrameTrain has no cloud storage for models.

faqs.q: Is there telemetry or tracking?
faqs.a: No automatic telemetry. FrameTrain does not collect usage data, training data, or model metrics. The only default network communication is license validation (API key check) at startup. You can optionally opt in to anonymized crash/error reporting in the app's settings to help us fix bugs – off by default, never includes training data, and can be disabled at any time.

faqs.q: How is my payment data protected?
faqs.a: Payment data is processed exclusively through Stripe, a PCI-DSS Level 1 certified payment service provider. We do not store any credit card data ourselves.

faqs.q: Which models can I train with FrameTrain?
faqs.a: All PyTorch-based HuggingFace models: LLMs (Llama, Mistral, Phi, GPT-2), text classifiers (BERT, RoBERTa, DistilBERT), sequence-to-sequence models (T5, BART), and many more.

faqs.q: Which file formats are supported for models?
faqs.a: PyTorch models (.pt, .bin), SafeTensors (.safetensors), and GGUF format (.gguf). Import from the HuggingFace Hub works directly via model ID.

faqs.q: How do I import models from HuggingFace?
faqs.a: In the Model Manager, simply enter the HuggingFace model ID (e.g. meta-llama/Llama-3-8B) and click "Download". FrameTrain downloads all necessary files and sets the model up automatically.

faqs.q: Can I use locally stored models?
faqs.a: Yes. In the Model Manager you can also provide the path to a locally stored model. Supported formats: HuggingFace format (model.safetensors / config.json), GGUF.

faqs.q: How do I export a trained model?
faqs.a: In the Versioning panel you can export any model version as SafeTensors, GGUF, or a PyTorch checkpoint. The export goes to your local machine, not the cloud.

lossProblems.intro: Common problems with training loss and their solutions:
items: Loss diverges (increases)
Learning rate too high → reduce to 1e-5 or 5e-6
Loss stops decreasing
Learning rate too low or dataset too small → increase rate or add more data
Validation loss much higher than train loss
Overfitting → more dropout, fewer epochs, or more data
Loss fluctuates wildly
Batch size too small → increase it or enable gradient clipping

faqs.q: What do training loss vs. validation loss mean?
faqs.a: Training loss measures the model's error on the training data; validation loss measures it on a separate test set. If the validation loss is much higher than the training loss, you have overfitting.

faqs.q: What do I do if the loss doesn't decrease?
faqs.a: __LOSS_PROBLEMS__

faqs.q: Which metrics does FrameTrain show during training?
faqs.a: Live metrics: training loss, validation loss, accuracy, learning rate, GPU utilization (VRAM, temp), elapsed/estimated time, gradient norm. All metrics are displayed as interactive charts.

faqs.q: What is gradient clipping?
faqs.a: Gradient clipping limits the maximum magnitude of gradients to prevent exploding gradients. FrameTrain sets max_grad_norm=1.0 by default. Reduce this value when you see loss spikes.

faqs.q: Are training histories saved?
faqs.a: Yes. All training sessions are saved locally in the training history. You can retrieve and compare metrics from past training runs.

faqs.q: How do I install FrameTrain on macOS?
faqs.a: 1. Download the .dmg file. 2. Open the DMG and drag the app to Applications. 3. Remove the quarantine flag: sudo xattr -cr '/Applications/FrameTrain.app'. 4. Launch the app. Detailed guide: /install

faqs.q: How do I install FrameTrain on Windows?
faqs.a: 1. Download the .msi or .exe file. 2. Right-click → Properties → check "Unblock". 3. Run the installer. 4. If SmartScreen warns you: "More info" → "Run anyway". Detailed guide: /install

faqs.q: How do I install FrameTrain on Linux?
faqs.a: 1. Install libfuse2: sudo apt install libfuse2t64 (Ubuntu 22.04+). 2. Download the .AppImage file. 3. Make it executable: chmod +x FrameTrain.AppImage. 4. Run: ./FrameTrain.AppImage. Detailed guide: /install

faqs.q: What is an API key and where do I find it?
faqs.a: The API key is your license key for the desktop app. You receive it after purchase on the payment page. It is entered once when the app starts and then stored locally.

faqs.q: The app won't start – what can I do?
faqs.a: Common causes: 1. API key not entered or invalid. 2. No internet connection for license validation. 3. macOS: quarantine flag still active (sudo xattr -cr). 4. Windows: file not unblocked (Properties → Unblock). For further help: support@frametrain.ai

faqs.q: How do I update FrameTrain?
faqs.a: FrameTrain has a built-in auto-updater. It automatically checks for a new version at startup. You can also check manually via Settings → Check for updates.

trainingBasics.heading: Training Basics
trainingBasics.subtitle: The most important concepts in ML training
trainingBasics.cyclesIntro: Epoch: One complete pass through the entire dataset. Batch: A subset of the dataset processed simultaneously. Steps: Number of parameter updates = dataset size / batch size.
trainingBasics.lossDesc: Measures the model's error. Lower loss = better predictions. Computed at every parameter update.
trainingBasics.accDesc: Measures the proportion of correct predictions. For classification: % of correctly classified examples. Goal: as high as possible.

items.name: Learning Rate
items.impact: How large the parameter updates are. Too high: training diverges. Too low: training is too slow.
items.tips: Start with 2e-4 for LoRA, 2e-5 for full fine-tuning

items.name: Batch Size
items.impact: How many examples are processed simultaneously. Larger batches = more stable updates but more VRAM.
items.tips: Halve the batch size if you run into VRAM issues

items.name: Epochs
items.impact: How many times the entire dataset is passed through. More epochs can lead to overfitting.
items.tips: 3-5 epochs is optimal for most tasks

items.name: LoRA Rank (r)
items.impact: Size of the LoRA matrices. Higher rank = more capacity but more VRAM and training time.
items.tips: r=16 is a good default. For limited VRAM: r=8

items.name: Max Seq Length
items.impact: Maximum token count per input. Longer sequences = more VRAM.
items.tips: Adjust to the average text length of your data

lora.heading: LoRA Training
lora.subtitle: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning
lora.whatText1: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) is a method for adapting language models with a fraction of the VRAM of full fine-tuning. Instead of updating all model parameters, LoRA inserts small "adapter" matrices.
lora.whatText2: These adapters are trained while the original base model stays frozen. Result: 70-90% less VRAM with comparable quality.
fullItems: Changes are permanently baked into the model

configItems.desc: Size of the low-rank matrices. Higher rank = more capacity. Default: 16
Scaling factor for the LoRA weights. Usually set to the rank value. Default: 32
Dropout rate for LoRA layers. Helps against overfitting on small datasets. Default: 0.05
Which layers are adapted. Default: q_proj, v_proj. More modules = more capacity

lora.successDesc: For 4-bit quantization (QLoRA), enable "4-bit Loading" in the Training Panel. Reduces VRAM requirements by ~50%.

meta.title: AI Training Coach – ML Basics, LoRA, Hyperparameters & More | FrameTrain
meta.description: The complete theory guide to machine learning training: neural networks, transformers, loss curves, LoRA/QLoRA, hyperparameter tuning, and advanced techniques.
meta.ogTitle: AI Training Coach – The Complete ML Theory Guide
meta.ogDescription: 8 chapters: from ML basics to advanced fine-tuning techniques.

finalCta.heading: Done! You now know all the basics
finalCta.text: From ML basics to advanced techniques like ensembles and mixed precision – you've made it through the whole AI Training Coach. Time to put what you've learned into practice.

hub.subtitle: Understand machine learning training from the ground up – from neural networks to LoRA fine-tuning. 8 chapters, completely free.

chapters.desc: The basics: what is ML, how do neural networks and transformers work, how does an AI actually learn.
The training process in detail: the loop, loss functions, metrics, and the train/val/test split.
Reading loss curves correctly: recognizing good training, overfitting, underfitting, and unstable training.
Diagnosis & fixes: concrete solutions for overfitting, underfitting, learning rate problems, and loss spikes.
Hyperparameters in detail: learning rate, schedulers, batch size, optimizer comparison, and regularization.
Fine-tuning methods compared: full fine-tuning, LoRA, QLoRA, other PEFT methods, and when to use what.
Dataset mastery: data quality, preprocessing, augmentation, and class balancing.

wasIstMl.title: What is Machine Learning?
wasIstMl.subtitle: The foundation of everything – understand before you train
wasIstMl.intro: Machine learning (ML) is a branch of artificial intelligence that enables computers to learn from experience. Instead of giving a computer exact rules, you show it example data – and it finds the patterns itself. The result is called a model.
wasIstMl.infoBoxText:
Classical: rules + data → answers
ML: data + answers → rules (the model)
With ML, the computer figures out the rules itself.

paradigms.title: Supervised Learning
paradigms.desc: You give the model data WITH correct answers (labels). It learns the mapping from input to output.

paradigms.title: Unsupervised Learning
paradigms.desc: The model only receives data WITHOUT labels. It searches on its own for patterns, clusters, and structures.

paradigms.title: Reinforcement Learning
paradigms.desc: The model learns through reward and punishment within an environment. It optimizes its strategy.

vocab.def: The "intelligence" – a mathematical function with millions of learned parameters that maps inputs to outputs.
The model's internal numbers. They are optimized during training and represent the model's "knowledge".
The process by which the model iteratively improves its parameters using example data.
Applying the fully trained model to new, unseen data.
The collection of training examples. Quality and quantity are both crucial.
One complete pass through ALL training data.
A small subset of the training data processed simultaneously.
A numeric value indicating how "wrong" the model currently is. Lower is better.
The mathematical direction in which parameters need to change to reduce the loss.
The model "memorizes" training data instead of generalizing – poor performance on new data.
The model is too simple or trained too briefly – poor performance on both training AND test data.
The ability to perform well on unseen data. The actual goal of training.

wasIstMl.whyFtHeading: Why fine-tuning instead of training from scratch?
wasIstMl.whyFtText: Training an LLM like GPT or LLaMA from scratch costs millions of euros and requires thousands of GPUs over weeks. With fine-tuning, you take an already pretrained model – one that already knows language, facts, and logic – and specialize it for your task. In hours instead of months, on your own GPU.
pretrainItems: ⏱ Weeks to months of compute time
💰 Hundreds of thousands to millions of euros
🖥 Hundreds to thousands of GPUs simultaneously
ftItems: 📦 A few hundred to a thousand examples are enough
wasIstMl.transferHeading: Transfer learning: the foundation of fine-tuning
wasIstMl.transferText: Fine-tuning is based on transfer learning: the prior knowledge of a large pretrained model (grammar, facts, logic, code) is transferred to a new, specific task. Only a fraction of the parameters need to be adapted.

transferDiagram.footnote: The base model already "knows" language. Fine-tuning teaches it your specific use case.

neuronaleNetze.title: Neural Networks
neuronaleNetze.subtitle: Understanding how modern AI models are built
neuronaleNetze.intro: An artificial neural network (ANN) is loosely inspired by the human brain. It consists of layers of neurons connected to one another. Each connection has a weight – the knowledge learned during training.
neuronaleNetze.neuronText: Each neuron receives inputs from all neurons in the previous layer, multiplies them by weights, sums everything up, and applies an activation function:
neuronaleNetze.neuronCode:
Neuron computation (simplified):
  output = activation(w₁·x₁ + w₂·x₂ + ... + wₙ·xₙ + b)

  w₁...wₙ = weights (learned during training)
  x₁...xₙ = inputs from the previous layer
  b        = bias (offset value)
  activation = non-linear function (ReLU, sigmoid, etc.)

Example with ReLU:
  output = max(0, w₁·x₁ + w₂·x₂ + b)
  → everything < 0 becomes 0 → the network learns "fires / doesn't fire"

activations.name: ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit)
activations.desc: The simplest and most common activation. Everything below 0 → 0, otherwise linear. Fast, little vanishing gradient.
activations.use: Standard in hidden layers of classic networks

activations.name: GeLU (Gaussian Error Linear Unit)
activations.desc: A smoother version of ReLU, used by BERT and GPT. Slightly better for transformer architectures.

activations.name: SiLU / Swish
activations.desc: Self-gated, smooth and differentiable everywhere. Used in newer architectures like LLaMA 2/3.

activations.name: Softmax
activations.desc: Converts raw scores into probabilities that sum to 1. Used only in the output layer for classification.
activations.use: Output layer for classification and LM head

neuronaleNetze.layersHeading: Key layer types in modern networks

layers.name: Dense / Fully Connected (Linear)
layers.desc: Every neuron connected to every neuron in the previous layer. Universal for classification and regression. In transformers: the FFN layers.

layers.name: Embedding Layer
layers.desc: Converts discrete tokens (words/characters) into continuous vectors. Fundamental for language models – the "representation" of each word.

layers.name: Multi-Head Attention
layers.desc: The core of transformers. Several parallel attention heads compute relationships between all token pairs in the sequence.

layers.name: Layer Normalization
layers.desc: Normalizes activations within a layer. Essential for stable transformer training. Prevents exploding/vanishing gradient issues.

layers.name: Dropout
layers.desc: Randomly disables neurons during training. Forces redundancy, acting as a strong regularizer against overfitting.

layers.name: Residual / Skip Connections
layers.desc: A direct connection from the input to the output of a block (x + f(x)). Enables very deep networks without gradient degradation.

neuronaleNetze.depthHeading: Depth vs. width: why depth is better
neuronaleNetze.depthText: A deep network (many layers) learns hierarchical representations: lower layers learn simple patterns (edges, syllables), middle layers combine them (shapes, words), upper layers recognize complex concepts (faces, meanings). Width alone isn't enough.

transformer.title: Transformers & LLMs
transformer.subtitle: The architecture behind GPT, LLaMA, BERT, and friends
transformer.intro: Since the paper "Attention Is All You Need" (Vaswani et al., 2017), the transformer architecture has dominated the AI world. All modern LLMs – GPT-4, LLaMA, Mistral, BERT – are based on it. The key: the self-attention mechanism.
transformer.infoBoxTitle: Revolutionary idea: only attention, no recurrence
transformer.infoBoxText: Before transformers, RNNs/LSTMs processed text word by word – slow and weak on long contexts. Transformers process all tokens simultaneously and can directly learn relationships across arbitrarily long distances.
transformer.attentionText: Self-attention lets every token in the text "look at" every other token to understand its context. Three projections (query, key, value) determine how strongly each token attends to others:
transformer.attentionCode:
For each token i:
  Query_i  = Token_i × W_Q    (what am I looking for?)
  Key_j    = Token_j × W_K    (what do I offer?)
  Value_j  = Token_j × W_V    (what is my content?)

Attention-Score(i,j) = softmax(Query_i · Key_j / √d_k)
Output_i = Σ_j [Attention-Score(i,j) × Value_j]

→ Each token gets an output that is a
  weighted combination of all other tokens.
transformer.multiHeadText: Instead of a single attention computation, h parallel heads are used. Each head learns to attend to a different type of relationship: head 1 → syntactic dependencies, head 2 → coreference, head 3 → semantic similarity, etc.

blockRows.name: Input Embeddings + Positional Encoding
blockRows.desc: Token → vector; position in the sentence encoded

transformer.vramIntro: Memory requirements for full fine-tuning (optimistic estimate with mixed precision):
transformer.vramConclusionTitle: The consequence: LoRA & QLoRA are game-changers
transformer.vramConclusionText:
Without LoRA: 7B fine-tuning = 28 GB VRAM (impossible on consumer GPUs).
With QLoRA: 7B fine-tuning = ~4 GB VRAM (an RTX 3070 is enough!).

wieKiLernt.title: How AI "learns"
wieKiLernt.subtitle: Fully understanding backpropagation and gradient descent
wieKiLernt.intro: Behind every training run are two fundamental algorithms: backpropagation (computes how wrong the model is and why) and gradient descent (adjusts the parameters in the right direction).

cycleSteps.title: Forward Pass
cycleSteps.desc: Training data flows forward through all layers. Each layer transforms the input until a prediction emerges.

cycleSteps.title: Compute the loss
cycleSteps.desc: The prediction is compared to the correct label. A loss function quantifies the error as a single number. The larger it is, the worse.

cycleSteps.title: Backward Pass (Backpropagation)
cycleSteps.desc: Using the chain rule of calculus, it's computed how much each individual parameter contributed to the total error. Result: a gradient for every parameter.

cycleSteps.title: Update the weights
cycleSteps.desc: The optimizer shifts each parameter against the gradient: W_new = W_old - LR × gradient. The step size is determined by the learning rate.

cycleSteps.title: Reset gradients
cycleSteps.desc: Gradients are reset to 0. Important! Otherwise gradients accumulate across batches (unless gradient accumulation is intentional).

cycleSteps.title: Repeat
cycleSteps.desc: This cycle repeats for every batch in every epoch. After thousands to millions of iterations, the model has "learned" the data.

wieKiLernt.lrHeading: The learning rate: the slider of learning
wieKiLernt.lrText: The learning rate (LR) determines how large each step downhill is. Too large: you overshoot the minimum and bounce around wildly. Too small: you almost never get there.
wieKiLernt.lrCode:
Weight update:
  W_new = W_old - α × ∂L/∂W

  α (alpha) = learning rate (e.g. 0.00002 = 2e-5)
  ∂L/∂W    = gradient (derivative of the loss w.r.t. W)

Example:
  W_old = 0.5, gradient = 0.3, LR = 0.01
  W_new = 0.5 - 0.01 × 0.3 = 0.5 - 0.003 = 0.497
wieKiLernt.sgdHeading: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) vs. Batch GD
wieKiLernt.minimaHeading: Why local minima aren't a problem (for large LLMs)
wieKiLernt.minimaText: In low-dimensional spaces (2D or 3D), there are many local minima you can get stuck in. In high-dimensional parameter spaces with billions of parameters, it's fundamentally different: almost all critical points are saddle points or flat regions, not true local minima. Moreover, all "good" local minima are about as good as the global minimum.
wieKiLernt.minimaConclusionText: When fine-tuning modern LLMs with AdamW and cosine decay, gradient descent is extremely stable. The real challenge isn't "which minimum will I land in", but "does training converge stably and quickly enough at all?".
wieKiLernt.gradientsText: In very deep networks, gradients can vanish during the backward pass (→ weights barely change, training stagnates) or explode (→ weights become huge, training breaks down). Modern architectures address this through:

trainingLoop.title: The Training Loop
trainingLoop.subtitle: What exactly happens when you click 'Start Training'?
trainingLoop.intro: When FrameTrain starts training, a precise, repeating cycle runs. Each of these cycles is called a step. Understanding this loop helps you spot problems, read logs, and set hyperparameters sensibly.
trainingLoop.loopCode:
For each epoch (1 to num_epochs):
  ┌─ For each batch (1 to dataset_size / batch_size):
  │   1. Load batch            → next B training examples
  │   2. Forward pass          → compute prediction
  │   3. Compute loss          → prediction vs. label
  │   4. Backward pass         → compute gradients (autograd)
  │   5. Gradient clipping     → max_grad_norm (optional, prevents explosion)
  │   6. optimizer.step()      → update weights
  │   7. optimizer.zero_grad() → reset gradients (!)
  │   8. scheduler.step()      → adjust LR (if scheduler active)
  └─ End batch

  After each epoch (or every N steps):
  9. Validation loop        → measure loss & metrics on val set
  10. Log metrics           → accuracy, F1, perplexity etc.
  11. Save checkpoint       → when best val performance is reached
  12. Check early stopping  → stop if no improvement
trainingLoop.modeIntro: PyTorch and all modern frameworks distinguish two modes. This difference is critical and is often forgotten:
trainingLoop.warningTitle: Common mistake: validation in the wrong mode
trainingLoop.warningText: If you forget to switch to eval() before validation, you get a lower validation loss than reality (dropout disables neurons in train mode). FrameTrain handles this correctly automatically.
trainingLoop.stepIntro: A step = 1 batch forward + backward + weight update. The total number of steps determines how much the model learns overall:
trainingLoop.stepCode:
Total Steps = ⌈dataset_size / batch_size⌉ × num_epochs

Example:
  Dataset:    1,000 training examples
  Batch Size: 8
  Epochs:     3

  Steps/Epoch  = ⌈1,000/8⌉ = 125
  Total Steps  = 125 × 3 = 375

Practical rule of thumb:
  For LLM fine-tuning: ~500–2,000 steps is usually enough
  For classification:  ~200–1,000 steps is often enough

checkpointStrategies.name: Best Checkpoint
checkpointStrategies.desc: Save only the model with the best validation loss. Saves disk space. Recommended for production.

checkpointStrategies.name: Every N Steps/Epochs
checkpointStrategies.desc: Save regularly, regardless of performance. Useful for resuming training after a crash.

checkpointStrategies.name: Last N Checkpoints
checkpointStrategies.desc: Keep only the last N checkpoints. For ensemble training or analyzing the training trajectory.

lossFunktionen.title: Loss Functions
lossFunktionen.subtitle: The training compass – which function for which task?
lossFunktionen.intro: The loss function is the heart of training: it measures how wrong the model currently is and gives the optimizer its direction. Choosing the right loss function is just as important as the model architecture.
lossFunktionen.mainHeading: The most important loss functions in detail

losses.name: Cross-Entropy Loss (CE)
losses.desc: The standard for all classification tasks. Penalizes wrong classes logarithmically – the more confidently wrong the model is, the harsher the penalty. Also used for next-token prediction in LLM training.
losses.example: Spam detection, sentiment analysis, all LLMs
losses.tip: In FrameTrain: chosen automatically for language models and classification.

losses.name: Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE)
losses.desc: A special case of CE for exactly two classes. Sigmoid output between 0 and 1. Good for yes/no decisions.
losses.tip: More efficient than CE with 2 classes, noticeable on large datasets.

losses.name: Mean Squared Error (MSE)
losses.desc: Measures the squared distance between prediction and target. Very sensitive to outliers (squared penalty). Smoother gradient than MAE.
losses.tip: If your dataset has outliers, prefer MAE or Huber loss.

losses.name: Mean Absolute Error (MAE)
losses.desc: Measures absolute distance. More robust than MSE with outliers because the penalty is linear instead of squared. Gradient is not differentiable at 0.
losses.example: Time series with outliers, financial data
losses.tip: Huber loss combines MSE (near the target) and MAE (far away) – the best of both.

lossFunktionen.clmText: The standard for all autoregressive decoder models (GPT, LLaMA, Mistral). Cross-entropy across all token positions: the model learns to predict the next token.
lossFunktionen.clmCode:
Input:  [BOS] "I love "
Target: "I love " [pizza]  ← predict the next token

Loss = -log P(pizza | "I love")
       + potentially further token positions
lossFunktionen.mlmText: For BERT-style encoders. 15% of tokens are masked, and the model must predict them. Bidirectional context – the model sees tokens before and after the masked token.
lossFunktionen.mlmCode:
Input:  "I [MASK] pizza very much."
Target: "I  love  pizza very much."  ← [MASK] = "love"

Loss = cross-entropy only on masked positions
lossFunktionen.autoBoxText: For LLMs (GPT, LLaMA, etc.): CLM loss. For BERT-style models: MLM loss. For classification heads: cross-entropy. You only need to choose the model and the task.

metriken.title: Metrics & Evaluation
metriken.subtitle: What do accuracy, F1, perplexity, and BLEU actually tell you?
metriken.intro: The loss is the internal training compass. Metrics are the human-interpretable performance indicators. A low loss doesn't guarantee good metrics – and vice versa.

metrics.name: Accuracy
metrics.desc: Proportion of all correct predictions. Easy to understand, but misleading on imbalanced datasets.
metrics.warning: Problem: with 99% negative examples, a model that ALWAYS says "negative" achieves 99% accuracy – and has learned nothing!

metrics.name: Precision
metrics.desc: Of all cases classified as positive: how many are actually positive? Important when false positives are costly (e.g. spam filter → no real email should be marked as spam).

metrics.name: Recall (Sensitivity)
metrics.desc: Of all true positive cases: how many were found? Important when false negatives are dangerous (e.g. disease detection → no real disease should be missed).

metrics.name: F1-Score
metrics.desc: The harmonic mean of precision and recall. The best single number for imbalanced datasets. Penalizes extreme imbalances between precision and recall.

metrics.name: AUC-ROC
metrics.desc: Measures the model's ability to separate classes, independent of the classification threshold. 1.0 = perfect, 0.5 = random.

metriken.pplText: The most important metric for language models. Measures how "surprised" the model is by the test data. Lower perplexity = better. A value of 10 means the model has to choose, on average, between ~10 equally likely tokens.
metriken.pplCode:
Perplexity = exp(Cross-Entropy Loss)

Examples:
  PPL = 5   → very good language model (confident predictions)
  PPL = 20  → mediocre
  PPL = 100 → poor model (very uncertain)
  PPL = e^0 = 1 → perfect prediction (impossible in practice)
metriken.bleuText: For translation and text generation. Compares generated text with reference text based on n-gram overlap (1-gram to 4-gram). Value between 0 and 1.
metriken.bleuGuidelines: Guidelines: BLEU > 0.4 = good translation, > 0.6 = very good, > 0.8 = nearly human
metriken.rougeText: ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, ROUGE-L measure recall of n-grams against reference summaries. Standard for summarization tasks.

whichTable.reason: Don't mark real emails as spam

trainValTest.title: Train / Validation / Test Split
trainValTest.subtitle: The golden rule of machine learning
trainValTest.intro: One of the most fundamental rules of ML: your dataset gets split into strictly separate parts. Without this separation, your metrics are worthless.

splits.name: 🏋️ Training set (60–80%)
splits.role: The model learns EXCLUSIVELY on this data. Weights are updated.
splits.rule: The only data the model is allowed to "see" while learning.

splits.name: 🔍 Validation set (10–20%)
splits.role: Measured after every epoch. Used for hyperparameter selection and early stopping. The model does NOT learn from it.
splits.rule: May be used multiple times, but not for learning!

splits.name: 🎯 Test set (10–20%)
splits.role: Used only ONCE, at the very end. Gives the honest final performance estimate.
splits.rule: The test set is sacred. Touch it only ONCE!
donts: Using it as a second validation set

trainValTest.leakageTitle: Data leakage – the most common and most damaging mistake
trainValTest.leakageText:
Definition: test or validation data "leaks" into the training data.
Consequence: metrics are optimistic and completely worthless. The model has "cheated".
Common cause: normalizing/standardizing over the entire dataset instead of just the training data. Correct approach: fit the scaler only on training data, then apply it to val/test.
trainValTest.cvIntro: With very little data, a single split can be unrepresentative. K-fold cross-validation helps:
trainValTest.cvCode:
K-Fold Cross-Validation (k=5):

  Fold 1: [████ Train ████ Train ████ Train ████ Train] [Val]
  Fold 2: [████ Train ████ Train ████ Train] [Val] [████ Train]
  Fold 3: [████ Train ████ Train] [Val] [████ Train ████ Train]
  ...

  → Train 5 models, average 5 val scores
  → More robust estimate of true performance
  → But: 5× the cost!

  Recommended when: dataset < 1,000 examples
trainValTest.stratText: With imbalanced classes, a stratified split ensures all splits have the same class distribution. Without stratification, a split can randomly end up with very few examples of a rare class in the validation set.
trainValTest.stratCode:
# Random split (bad with imbalance):
  Train: 95% negative, 5% positive
  Val:   80% negative, 20% positive  ← randomly unrepresentative!

# Stratified split (always recommended):
  Train: 95% negative, 5% positive
  Val:   95% negative, 5% positive  ← same distribution ✓
trainValTest.recommendationText: Always use stratified split. On small datasets (<1,000 examples), consider K-fold cross-validation. Never use the test set for hyperparameter decisions.

lossKurven.title: Interpreting Loss Curves
lossKurven.subtitle: The heart of training monitoring
lossKurven.intro: The loss curve is your most important diagnostic tool. A trained eye can tell within seconds whether training is healthy, problems are emerging, or immediate action is needed.

anatomyItems.title: X-axis: Time
anatomyItems.desc: Training steps or epochs. Further right means longer training. Steps give more granularity.

anatomyItems.title: Y-axis: Loss value
anatomyItems.desc: The model's error. Lower = better. Goal: drop to a low, stable level as quickly as possible.

anatomyItems.title: Training Loss (purple)
anatomyItems.desc: Error on training data. Calculated after every batch. Often somewhat noisy due to stochasticity.

anatomyItems.title: Validation Loss (dashed)
anatomyItems.desc: Error on validation data. Smoother than training loss. The decisive indicator of real performance.

lossKurven.goodText: Both curves drop steadily and converge to a low value. Small, stable gap between training and validation loss. It flattens out at the end (plateau).
lossKurven.overfittingText: Training loss keeps dropping, validation loss starts to rise. The divergence point marks the optimal training time (early stopping!)
lossKurven.underfittingText: Both curves stagnate at a high level. The model isn't learning the patterns. Causes: LR too low, too few epochs, wrong model.
lossKurven.highLrText: Strong oscillation, no convergence. The optimizer keeps overshooting the minimum. Solution: reduce LR.
lossKurven.benchmarksNote: Note: Absolute values depend heavily on task and model. Trend and comparison matter more than absolute numbers.
lossKurven.frametrainText: In the FrameTrain monitoring panel you see training loss and validation loss in real time. The dashboard shows both steps and epochs on the X-axis. Base your actions on the validation loss — not the training loss!

gutesTraining.title: Recognizing Good Training
gutesTraining.subtitle: The signs of successful, stable training
gutesTraining.idealText: Training loss and validation loss both drop steadily, stay close together (small generalization gap), and flatten out at the end on a low, stable plateau.

checklist.check: Both losses drop noticeably in the first epochs
checklist.detail: Shows active learning. If losses barely drop from epoch 1 → underfitting, LR too low, or a data problem.

checklist.check: Training loss ≈ validation loss (small, stable gap)
checklist.detail: Typically val loss is 5–15% higher. A huge gap means overfitting. No gap means underfitting or identical data (data leakage!).

checklist.check: Curves flatten out toward the end (plateau)
checklist.detail: The model is approaching its ceiling on this dataset. Normal and desired.

checklist.check: No large jumps (spikes) in the loss
checklist.detail: Occasional small spikes are normal. Large, recurring spikes mean LR too high or faulty data.

checklist.check: Validation accuracy rises monotonically or nearly so
checklist.detail: Small fluctuations are normal. A long-term upward trend shows real improvement.

checklist.check: Validation loss reaches a minimum and stabilizes
checklist.detail: That's the optimal checkpoint. The ideal model is saved here.

phases.desc: Loss drops steeply. The model learns the most obvious patterns. Validation loss closely follows training loss.
Loss drops more slowly. Subtler patterns are learned. The generalization gap may widen slightly.
Barely any more improvement. Training loss minimally below validation loss. A good time to stop.
Training loss keeps dropping, validation loss rises. From here: load the best checkpoint.

whenTable.signal: Validation loss flattens out, only minimal fluctuation (±0.001)
whenTable.action: Give it 2–3 more epochs of patience, then stop. Load the best checkpoint.

whenTable.signal: Validation loss rises for the first time
whenTable.action: That was the best epoch. Load the best checkpoint and stop.

whenTable.signal: Validation loss rises for 3 epochs in a row
whenTable.action: Early stopping triggered. The best checkpoint is optimal.
Overfitting. Either revert to the best checkpoint or abort training.

overfitting.title: Recognizing Overfitting
overfitting.subtitle: When the model memorizes the training data
overfitting.intro: Overfitting is the most common problem when training deep networks. The model learns the training data so well that it also memorizes noise and random artifacts. On new, unseen data it then performs noticeably worse.

signs.sign: Training loss keeps dropping, validation loss rises
Large, growing gap between training and validation loss
Training accuracy near 100%, validation accuracy noticeably lower
Model performs very well on training data, poorly on new data
Validation loss forms a U-curve after initially dropping

causes.detail: Few examples means the model memorizes them instead of learning patterns. Solution: collect more data or use augmentation.
Long training grinds the training data in ever deeper. Early stopping would have been the solution.

causes.cause: Model too complex for the amount of data
causes.detail: Too many parameters for little data means too much capacity for memorization. Use a smaller model or LoRA with a small rank.
Without regularization the model learns more freely. Apply more dropout or weight decay.
Dataset contains duplicates or very similar examples. Deduplication helps.
Test data ended up in training: the model has "cheated". Metrics look good, real performance is poor.

overfitting.tradeoffIntro: Overfitting and underfitting are the two sides of the classic bias-variance tradeoff:
overfitting.infoText: A small gap between training loss and validation loss is always present and normal. It only becomes a problem once the gap is large AND validation loss is rising. In LoRA fine-tuning overfitting is rarer, since only a few parameters are trained.

underfitting.title: Recognizing Underfitting
underfitting.subtitle: When the model doesn't learn the patterns
underfitting.intro: Underfitting means the model fails to capture the underlying patterns in the data. It performs poorly on both training and validation data. It's the opposite of overfitting.

signs.sign: High training loss that barely changes across epochs
signs.detail: The worst sign — the model is learning practically nothing.

signs.sign: High validation loss correlates with training loss (both high)
signs.detail: Both curves lie close together, but at a high level.

signs.sign: Low training accuracy (< 60–70% on simple tasks)
signs.detail: Can't even classify its own training data properly.

signs.sign: Loss curve flattens very early without dropping much
signs.detail: Stagnation after a few steps — no further learning progress.

signs.sign: Val loss and train loss are nearly identical, but both high
signs.detail: No overfitting, but also no learning — the model is too simple.

causes.fix: Increase LR by a factor of 3–10. Start at 1e-5, try 3e-5, then 1e-4.

causes.check: Training loss still dropping at the last epoch
causes.fix: Double the epochs. The loss curve shows whether there's still potential.

causes.check: Even long training doesn't improve it
causes.fix: Choose a larger base model: BERT → RoBERTa → DeBERTa, or a 7B model instead of 1B.
Use AdamW instead of SGD. AdamW is the standard for all transformer training.

causes.check: Train and val loss both high and similar
causes.fix: Set dropout to 0.0 for testing, then increase gradually.

causes.check: Loss is constant from the start (no drop)
causes.fix: Check the dataset format carefully: is tokenization correct? Are labels correct (0-indexed vs. 1-indexed)?

causes.cause: Vanishing gradients in very deep networks
causes.fix: Check layer normalization, ensure residual connections, review initialization.

underfitting.warningText: Train briefly (1–2 epochs) on a very small subset (~50 examples). A well-configured model should reach nearly 100% training accuracy on 50 examples (overfitting on the mini-dataset). If not → check the underfitting issue against data format or LR.

instabilesTraining.title: Unstable Training
instabilesTraining.subtitle: Diagnosing loss spikes, explosions, and chaos
instabilesTraining.intro: Unstable training shows up as strong fluctuations, sudden jumps, or a complete loss crash. These patterns have clear causes and solvable fixes.
instabilesTraining.patternsHeading: Instability patterns and what they mean

patterns.pattern: Strong oscillation: loss jumps up and down every epoch
patterns.desc: The optimizer keeps "jumping" past the minimum and oscillates further. No convergence possible.
patterns.fix: Reduce LR by a factor of 5–10. Add an LR warmup.

patterns.desc: A faulty or extremely difficult batch caused a temporary spike. Gradient clipping helps.
patterns.fix: Enable gradient clipping (max_grad_norm=1.0). Check the dataset for outliers.

patterns.pattern: Loss explodes and becomes NaN or Inf
patterns.desc: Gradients grow exponentially until numeric values become unstable. Training crashes.
patterns.fix: Set gradient clipping to 1.0. Drastically reduce LR. Disable mixed precision or switch to fp32.

patterns.pattern: Loss suddenly rises after many good epochs
patterns.cause: LR spike from a wrong scheduler / warmup too long
patterns.desc: A misconfigured LR scheduler can suddenly increase the LR. Or: a very unusual batch after long stability.
patterns.fix: Check the scheduler configuration. Load the best checkpoint. Gradient clipping.

patterns.pattern: Regular, gentle waves in the loss
patterns.desc: Not a problem! Cyclical LR schedulers intentionally produce cyclical loss patterns. Each cycle converges anew.
patterns.fix: Only if undesired: choose a different scheduler. Otherwise: expected behavior.

instabilesTraining.clipHeading: Gradient clipping – the most important safeguard
instabilesTraining.clipIntro: Gradient clipping caps the maximum norm of the gradients. If gradients get too large, they're scaled proportionally. Default value: max_grad_norm = 1.0
instabilesTraining.clipCode:
Without gradient clipping:
  Gradient = [0.01, 0.02, ..., 500.0]  ← explosion!
  → weight update huge → training crashes (NaN)

With gradient clipping (max_norm = 1.0):
  Gradient norm = sqrt(sum(g²)) = 500.01
  If norm > 1.0: gradient = gradient × (1.0 / norm)
  → gradient is scaled, direction is preserved
  → safe update, training stays stable

In FrameTrain: set to 1.0 by default.
instabilesTraining.successText: Gradient clipping is enabled by default in FrameTrain (max_grad_norm = 1.0). This catches most explosion problems automatically. If NaN loss still occurs: reduce LR and check the data for outliers.

overfittingFix.title: Fighting Overfitting
overfittingFix.subtitle: Concrete measures sorted by effectiveness
overfittingFix.intro: Overfitting is the most common problem in training. The good news: it's well researched and there are many proven techniques. Used alone or combined, they solve practically every overfitting case.
overfittingFix.measuresHeading: The 7 most effective measures against overfitting

measures.name: More diverse training data
measures.desc: More and more diverse data is always the most effective measure. More examples means the model can't memorize individual ones.
measures.how: Grow the dataset (real data), data augmentation (chapter 7), generate synthetic data via LLM.

measures.name: Enable early stopping
measures.desc: Stop training before overfitting begins. Monitor validation loss and stop once it stops dropping.
measures.how: patience=3–5, monitor="val_loss", always save the best checkpoint.

measures.name: Use LoRA with a small rank
measures.desc: LoRA drastically limits the number of trainable parameters. Fewer free parameters means less overfitting capacity.
measures.how: rank=4 or rank=8 instead of rank=32. Only q_proj and v_proj as target_modules.

measures.name: Increase dropout
measures.desc: A higher dropout value randomly deactivates more neurons. The network has to learn more robust, redundant representations.
measures.how: Increase LoRA dropout from 0.05 to 0.1–0.2. Watch out: too high → underfitting.

measures.name: Train for fewer epochs
measures.desc: The simplest solution: just stop earlier. Useful when early stopping reacts too late.
measures.how: Halve the epochs. The validation loss curve shows where the turning point was.

measures.name: Increase weight decay (L2 regularization)
measures.desc: Penalizes large weights. Keeps model parameters small and prevents the model from over-weighting individual features.
measures.how: Increase weight_decay from 0.01 to 0.05–0.1 in the AdamW optimizer.

measures.name: Enable label smoothing
measures.desc: Instead of hard 0/1 labels, soft labels are used (e.g. 0.9/0.05). Prevents overconfident predictions.
measures.how: label_smoothing=0.1. Good if the model is too confident.

combos.combo: Early stopping + LoRA rank=4–8 + weight_decay=0.1 + dropout=0.1
combos.prio: Data limitation is the main problem

combos.combo: Early stopping + small LR (1e-5) + weight_decay=0.1 + dropout=0.2
combos.prio: Be careful! Full FT with little data means high overfitting risk

overfittingFix.goldenRuleText: Early stopping + LoRA (rank=8–16) + weight_decay=0.01 is the most effective and simplest combination. It covers 90% of all overfitting cases.

underfittingFix.title: Fixing Underfitting
underfittingFix.subtitle: Step-by-step diagnosis and solutions
underfittingFix.intro: Underfitting is often easier to fix than overfitting. The first step is always to identify the exact cause — then the right fix follows.

treeSteps.question: Does the training loss drop at all in the first 100 steps?
treeSteps.no: NO → immediately: check LR (likely too low), check dataset format (labels correct?)

treeSteps.question: Is the training loss still dropping at the last epoch without a plateau?
treeSteps.yes: Yes → train for more epochs (stopped too early)

treeSteps.question: Can the model overfit a mini-dataset (50 examples)?
treeSteps.yes: Yes (100% train acc) → increase data amount, more epochs, check LR
treeSteps.no: NO → fundamental problem: LR, model size, or data format is wrong

treeSteps.question: Is dropout too high or regularization too strong?
treeSteps.yes: Yes → set dropout to 0 to test, reduce weight decay
treeSteps.no: No → model too small, wrong optimizer, or LR still wrong

fixes.fix: Increase LR step by step: 1e-6 → 1e-5 → 5e-5 → 1e-4. Short training run each time to test.
Double or triple the epochs. Watch the loss curve: is it still dropping?
Larger model: 1B → 7B, or a more specialized model for the domain.
AdamW is the standard. SGD without warmup is almost always wrong for transformers.
Manually check samples. Are labels correct? Is tokenization correct? Encoding issues?
Temporarily set dropout to 0.0. If the model then learns: dropout was too high.

fixes.cause: Batch size too small / gradients too noisy
fixes.fix: Increase batch size or use gradient accumulation. More stable gradients.

underfittingFix.miniDatasetTitle: The mini-dataset test – your most important debugging tool
underfittingFix.miniDatasetText: Train for 10 epochs on 50–100 examples. The model MUST overfit this data (100% training accuracy). If not: fundamental problem with LR, data format, or model. If yes: the configuration is fundamentally correct, more data and early stopping will solve the rest.

lrProbleme.title: Learning Rate Problems
lrProbleme.subtitle: Fully diagnosing the most common source of errors
lrProbleme.intro: The learning rate is the most sensitive of all hyperparameters. Being off by a factor of 10, too high or too low, can mean the difference between perfect training and complete failure.

matrixRows.symptom: Loss drops well, then suddenly spikes

lrProbleme.rangeTestHeading: LR range test – the best method for finding LR
lrProbleme.rangeTestIntro: The LR range test is the most systematic method: train for 1 epoch with exponentially increasing LR and plot the loss. The optimal LR sits just before the steepest descent.
lrProbleme.rangeTestCode:
LR range test procedure:
  1. Start with a very small LR (e.g. 1e-8)
  2. Increase LR exponentially each step: LR × 1.3
  3. Plot loss vs. LR
  4. Find the point of steepest loss descent
  5. Optimal LR ≈ 1/10 of that point

  Example:
    Steepest descent at LR = 1e-4
    → Good starting LR ≈ 1e-5

  Rule of thumb for transformer fine-tuning:
    BERT family:    2e-5 to 5e-5
    GPT/decoder:    1e-5 to 1e-4
    LoRA (LLaMA):   1e-4 to 3e-4
lrProbleme.batchHeading: LR and batch size: the relationship
lrProbleme.batchIntro: A larger batch size means more stable gradients, which allows a higher LR. The linear scaling rule:
lrProbleme.batchCode:
If batch size × 2 → LR × 2 (linear scaling)

Example:
  Batch size 8, LR = 2e-5 (baseline)
  Batch size 16 → LR ≈ 4e-5
  Batch size 32 → LR ≈ 8e-5

Warning: at very large batch sizes (> 512)
  linear scaling breaks down → square root scaling is better:
  LR ∝ sqrt(batch size)
lrProbleme.warmupHeading: Warmup: why it matters so much
lrProbleme.warmupIntro: At the start of training, gradients are unreliable (random initialization). An LR warmup starts with a very small LR and increases it linearly to the target LR:
lrProbleme.warmupCode:
Without warmup: immediate large update on unreliable gradients
              → instability, possible early divergence

With warmup:
  Steps 1–100:  LR rises from 0 → 2e-5 (linear)
  Steps 101+:   LR at 2e-5 or cosine decay

Recommendation: warmup = 5–10% of total steps
  For 1000 total steps: 50–100 warmup steps
lrProbleme.recommendationText: For most fine-tuning jobs: start with 2e-5 for BERT-like models, 2e-4 for LoRA. If unstable: halve it. If too slow: double it. Always enable warmup.

lossSpike.title: Loss Spikes & Crashes
lossSpike.subtitle: Understanding and fixing sudden outliers during training
lossSpike.intro: A loss spike is a sudden, sharp increase in loss during training. Training can recover afterward — or crash completely (gradient explosion).

types.symptoms: Loss jumps 2–10× higher for 1–3 steps, then back to trend
solutions: Check the dataset for faulty entries (extremely short/long texts)
If frequent: a data quality check is strongly recommended

types.cause: LR too high without sufficient clipping
types.symptoms: Recurring large spikes, general instability, no progress
solutions: Reduce LR by a factor of 5–10

types.type: Loss explodes to NaN or Inf
types.symptoms: Loss becomes NaN, training crashes or errors out
solutions: Switch mixed precision to fp32 (disable bf16/fp16)
Check layer normalization in the model
Check the dataset for extremely rare characters / encoding errors

types.cause: Usually POSITIVE: the model learned an important pattern (phase transition)
types.symptoms: Loss drops to a new, lower level in a short time and stays there
solutions: Wait and see! Usually not a problem
If it rises again afterward: it was a real spike → normal behavior after clipping
If it stays unstable: reduce LR slightly

preventions.name: Gradient clipping
preventions.desc: Limits the maximum gradient norm. Protection number 1 against explosions.

preventions.name: LR warmup
preventions.desc: Prevents early spikes from unreliable gradients at the start.

preventions.name: Data cleaning
preventions.desc: Faulty or extreme data points are common causes of spikes.

preventions.name: bf16 instead of fp16
preventions.desc: BFloat16 has a larger numeric range, less overflow risk than fp16.

lossSpike.defaultsText: Gradient clipping (1.0), warmup (100 steps), bf16 (if GPU supports it), and data validation are enabled by default in FrameTrain. For NaN loss: first check the data for faulty characters and encoding issues.

learningRateDeep.title: Learning Rate (In Depth)
learningRateDeep.subtitle: Fully understanding the most important hyperparameter
learningRateDeep.intro: The learning rate (LR) determines the step size in gradient descent. It's the most influential hyperparameter: no other parameter can affect training as strongly, positively or negatively.
learningRateDeep.recommendationsHeading: LR recommendations by model type and task

recommendations.note: Decoder models tolerate a somewhat higher LR
Higher rank = more parameters = lower LR
Similar to LoRA, possibly a bit higher
Low due to catastrophic forgetting risk
Much higher: all parameters must learn from zero

learningRateDeep.rangeTestHeading: The LR range test – finding it systematically
learningRateDeep.rangeTestCode:
LR range test (implementation):

  1. Initialize the model fresh
  2. Train for 1 epoch with LR schedule: 1e-8 → 1e-1
     (exponentially increasing over all steps)
  3. Plot loss vs. LR (log scale)
  4. Identify:
     - Point where loss drops most steeply → LR_steep
     - Point where loss starts to rise → LR_max
  5. Good LR = LR_steep / 10 to LR_steep

Example result:
  LR_steep  = 5e-4
  LR_max    = 2e-3
  → Good LR = 5e-5 (safely below steep)
             or 1e-4 (more aggressive)
learningRateDeep.scalingCode:
Linear scaling rule:
  LR_new = LR_base × (batch_size_new / batch_size_base)

Example:
  Base: BS=8, LR=2e-5
  New:  BS=32 → LR = 2e-5 × (32/8) = 8e-5

For batch size < 512:  linear scaling works well
For batch size > 512:  square root scaling is better:
  LR_new = LR_base × sqrt(BS_new / BS_base)

Note: often optional for small changes (8→16).
learningRateDeep.practicalText: Always start with the default value of the chosen model. If loss barely drops after 1–2 epochs: LR × 3. If loss oscillates: LR ÷ 5. Always set warmup to 5–10% of the steps.

lrScheduler.title: LR Scheduler Strategies
lrScheduler.subtitle: Intelligently steering the learning rate during training
lrScheduler.intro: An LR scheduler automatically adjusts the LR during training. Instead of a constant LR, it enables fast learning at the start and precise fine-tuning toward the end.
lrScheduler.schedulersHeading: The 5 most important schedulers in detail

schedulers.name: Warmup + Cosine Decay ⭐ (Gold standard)
schedulers.desc: LR rises linearly from 0 to the target LR (warmup), then a gentle cosine curve down to nearly 0.
schedulers.use: Gold standard for all LLM fine-tuning jobs
schedulers.pros: Gentle cooldown at the end. Very stable, reproducible results. Widely used and well proven.
schedulers.cons: Warmup steps must be defined in advance.

schedulers.name: Warmup + Linear Decay
schedulers.desc: LR rises linearly (warmup), then a linear decline to 0.
schedulers.cons: Cosine decay is marginally better for LLMs.

schedulers.name: Warmup + Constant
schedulers.desc: LR rises linearly to the target LR, then stays constant.
schedulers.use: Short fine-tuning jobs, when convergence isn't critical
schedulers.cons: No automatic cooldown – can become unstable toward the end.

schedulers.name: Cosine Annealing with Warm Restarts (SGDR)
schedulers.desc: Repeats cosine decay cycles with restarts. After each cycle: new exploration.
schedulers.use: Ensemble training, when multiple good checkpoints are desired
schedulers.pros: Diverse checkpoints from different minima. Helpful for ensembles.
schedulers.cons: More complex. The loss curve looks cyclical – can be confusing.

schedulers.name: ReduceLROnPlateau
schedulers.desc: LR is automatically reduced when validation loss doesn't drop for N epochs.
schedulers.use: When the optimal number of epochs is unknown, exploratory training
schedulers.cons: Can react too early or too late. Needs validation loss after every epoch.

lrScheduler.warmupCode:
Calculating warmup steps:
  warmup_steps = total_steps × warmup_ratio (e.g. 0.06)

total_steps = (dataset_size / batch_size) × num_epochs

Examples:
  1,000 examples, BS=8, 3 epochs:
    total_steps = 125 × 3 = 375
    warmup_steps = 375 × 0.06 = ~23 steps

  5,000 examples, BS=16, 2 epochs:
    total_steps = 313 × 2 = 625
    warmup_steps = 625 × 0.06 = ~38 steps

Rule of thumb: 50–200 warmup steps for most jobs

batchSizeDeep.title: Batch Size & Gradient Accumulation
batchSizeDeep.subtitle: Balancing efficiency, memory, and quality
batchSizeDeep.intro: The batch size determines how many training examples are processed simultaneously before weights are updated. It directly affects memory requirements, training speed, and often generalization quality.

largeItems.text: Can generalize worse at very large BS

batchSizeDeep.sweetSpotText: An effective batch size of 16–32 is ideal for most fine-tuning jobs. Going larger often brings no further benefit with constant LR scaling. Smaller than 8 can destabilize training.
batchSizeDeep.gradAccIntro: Gradient accumulation resolves the dilemma between limited VRAM and a large effective batch size: you process small batches, accumulate gradients over several steps, and only then perform a weight update.
batchSizeDeep.gradAccCode:
Gradient accumulation example:

  Goal: effective batch size = 32, but only 6 GB VRAM

  Batch size:                4  (fits in VRAM)
  Gradient accumulation:     8  (accumulate over 8 steps)
  Effective batch size:   4×8 = 32  ✓

  Procedure:
    Step 1: Forward(Batch1) → accumulate gradient ÷8
    Step 2: Forward(Batch2) → accumulate gradient ÷8
    ...
    Step 8: Forward(Batch8) → accumulate gradient ÷8
            → optimizer.step()  (1 update from 32 samples)
            → optimizer.zero_grad()

  Important: divide gradients by accumulation_steps!
  Otherwise: updates become too large

vramTable.note: RTX 4080/A4000 – full FT of smaller models

batchSizeDeep.effectiveCode:
Effective batch size = batch_size × gradient_accumulation_steps
                         × num_gpus  (for multi-GPU)

Example (1 GPU):
  batch_size = 4, accumulation = 8, gpus = 1
  → effective = 4 × 8 = 32

Recommendation: aim for an effective BS of 16–32
  If BS 32 doesn't fit in VRAM:
  → BS=4, accumulation=8 is equivalent

optimizerVergleich.title: Optimizer Comparison
optimizerVergleich.subtitle: Which optimizer for which situation?
optimizerVergleich.intro: The optimizer determines how gradients are turned into weight updates. Besides the LR, it's the most important factor affecting training speed, stability, and final performance.
optimizerVergleich.optimizersHeading: The most important optimizers in detail

optimizers.name: AdamW ⭐ (Standard)
optimizers.use: Standard for all modern transformer models
optimizers.desc: Combines adaptive learning (Adam) with correctly decoupled weight decay. The difference from Adam: weight decay is applied directly to weights, not folded into the gradients. Result: better regularization.
optimizers.pros: Stable, fast, robust. Works out of the box for almost all LLM fine-tuning jobs.
optimizers.cons: Needs ~3× the memory of the model weights (moment statistics). For 7B = ~14 GB extra.
optimizers.rec: ⭐ Always use AdamW as the first attempt

optimizers.name: AdamW 8-bit (bitsandbytes)
optimizers.use: Memory optimization for large models on consumer GPUs
optimizers.desc: Quantizes the Adam moment statistics to 8-bit. Saves ~75% optimizer memory with minimal quality loss. Essential for full fine-tuning on 24GB GPUs.
optimizers.pros: Cuts optimizer memory to a third. Quality nearly identical to fp32 AdamW.
optimizers.cons: Requires the bitsandbytes library. Minimally slower due to dequantization.
optimizers.rec: Use when GPU memory is tight for full fine-tuning

optimizers.name: SGD with Momentum
optimizers.use: Computer vision, sometimes better than Adam for CV
optimizers.desc: The simplest optimizer with momentum. Well understood and theoretically grounded. Often better for vision models (ResNet, etc.), but rarely better than AdamW for NLP/LLMs.
optimizers.pros: Simple, less memory than Adam (no moment statistics). Theoretically well understood.
optimizers.cons: Very sensitive to LR. Needs careful LR tuning. Almost always worse than AdamW for LLMs.

optimizers.name: Adafactor
optimizers.formula: Adam variant with factorized covariance matrix
optimizers.use: Very large models (T5, GPT-3 scale) where AdamW OOMs
optimizers.desc: Saves memory through factorized instead of full moment statistics. For very large models where AdamW needs too much memory. Standard for Google models (T5, PaLM).
optimizers.pros: Dramatically less memory than AdamW. Scales to very large models.
optimizers.cons: Requires more careful LR tuning. Convergence sometimes worse than AdamW.

optimizerVergleich.memoryCode:
Memory overview (for a 7B model, bf16):

  Model weights (bf16):    ~14 GB
  Activations (BS=4):      ~4 GB
  Gradients:                ~14 GB (same as weights)
  Optimizer states:
    AdamW (fp32):          ~28 GB (2× weights for moments)
    AdamW 8bit:            ~7 GB  (1/4 of AdamW fp32)
    SGD:                   ~14 GB (only 1× moment)
    Adafactor:             ~2 GB  (factorized)

  Total (AdamW):   14+4+14+28 = ~60 GB → needs an A100!
  Total (AdamW8b): 14+4+14+7  = ~39 GB → 2× RTX 3090 or A100
  With LoRA (no gradient for frozen layers):
    Total (AdamW LoRA): ~14+1+1+2 = ~18 GB → RTX 3090!

regularisierung.title: Regularization
regularisierung.subtitle: Techniques for better generalization and stable training
regularisierung.intro: Regularization covers techniques that prevent the model from "memorizing" the training data, encouraging better generalization instead.

techniques.name: L2 Regularization (Weight Decay)
techniques.desc: Adds a term to the loss proportional to the square of the weights. Penalizes large weights and keeps the model "modest". Correctly decoupled in AdamW.
techniques.effect: Prevents extreme weight values. Implicit "Occam's razor" – simpler solutions are favored.
techniques.tip: Increase weight_decay to 0.05–0.1 under strong overfitting.

techniques.name: Dropout
techniques.formula: during training: h_i = 0 with probability p
techniques.desc: Randomly deactivates neurons during training. The network can't rely on individual neurons and learns redundant representations.
techniques.effect: The strongest regularization tool. Prevents co-adaptation of neurons.
techniques.tip: Only active during training! At inference: all neurons active (scaled).

techniques.name: Label Smoothing
techniques.desc: Instead of hard 0/1 labels, soft targets are used (e.g. 0.9 for positive, 0.1/(K-1) for all others). Prevents an overconfident model.
techniques.effect: Improves calibration (confidence = true probability). Slight overfitting reduction.
techniques.tip: Especially helpful for NLP tasks with a lot of label noise.

techniques.name: Gradient Clipping
techniques.desc: Limits the maximum gradient norm. Not really a regularizer, but stabilizes training and prevents explosions.
techniques.effect: Prevents gradient explosion and NaN loss. Essential for large models.
techniques.tip: Standard in FrameTrain: always enabled. For very stable trainings: 0.5 for stricter clipping.

techniques.name: Early Stopping
techniques.formula: Stop when val_loss >= best_val_loss for patience epochs
techniques.desc: Ends training once validation loss stops improving. Implicitly prevents overfitting from training too long.
techniques.effect: Prevents the overfitting phase through a timely stop. Loads the best checkpoint.
techniques.tip: Always combine with save_best_only=True for automatic best-checkpoint saving.

regularisierung.combineCode:
Recommended combination for FrameTrain fine-tuning:

  # Standard (little overfitting):
  weight_decay = 0.01
  dropout      = 0.05 (LoRA dropout)
  early_stopping patience = 3

  # Moderate (medium overfitting):
  weight_decay = 0.05
  dropout      = 0.1
  label_smoothing = 0.05
  early_stopping patience = 2

  # Strong (heavy overfitting, little data):
  weight_decay = 0.1
  dropout      = 0.2
  label_smoothing = 0.1
  early_stopping patience = 2
  lora_rank    = 4 (fewer trainable parameters)

fullFinetuning.title: Full Fine-Tuning
fullFinetuning.subtitle: Adjusting all model parameters – maximum control
fullFinetuning.intro: In full fine-tuning, all parameters of the base model are trained and adjusted. Maximum expressiveness, but maximum memory requirements.
fullFinetuning.forgettingText: During full fine-tuning, the model can "forget" its general prior knowledge if trained too aggressively on new data. This happens when new knowledge overwrites the old weights.
fullFinetuning.forgettingCode:
Countermeasures against catastrophic forgetting:

  1. Low learning rate (1e-5 or lower)
     → Small updates preserve existing knowledge

  2. Few epochs (1–3)
     → Don't grind the model too long into new data

  3. Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC)
     → Penalizes large changes to important parameters
     → More complex, but effective

  4. Replay (mixed training)
     → Mix of old (general) and new (specific) data
     → e.g. 80% new task + 20% general text
fullFinetuning.comparisonHeading: Full fine-tuning vs. LoRA: when to use which?

comparisonRows.reason: Full FT doesn't fit in memory

comparisonRows.condition: Multiple task-specific versions of a model
comparisonRows.reason: Load base model once, swap adapters

comparisonRows.condition: Model needs to learn fundamentally new knowledge
comparisonRows.reason: Adjusting all layers for deep changes

fullFinetuning.checklistText: Before starting full fine-tuning: ✓ VRAM ≥ 24 GB, ✓ bf16 enabled, ✓ LR at most 5e-5, ✓ at most 3 epochs planned, ✓ early stopping enabled, ✓ best-checkpoint saving on.

loraDeep.title: LoRA In Depth
loraDeep.subtitle: Low-Rank Adaptation – the key to efficient fine-tuning
loraDeep.intro: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation), introduced in 2022 by Hu et al. (Microsoft), is the most important technique for efficient LLM fine-tuning. It reduces trainable parameters by 99%+ with minimal performance loss.
loraDeep.mathText: Instead of changing the entire weight matrix W (d×d), LoRA freezes W and adds two small matrices A (d×r) and B (r×d) whose product represents the change:
loraDeep.mathCode:
Standard fine-tuning:
  W_new = W + ΔW    (ΔW is the same size as W!)
  7B model: ΔW ≈ 7 billion parameters to train

LoRA:
  W_new = W + B·A   (W stays frozen)
  A: d×r  (down-projection)
  B: r×d  (up-projection)

Example (d=4096, r=16):
  Original matrix:  4096 × 4096 = 16.7M parameters
  LoRA matrices:     4096×16 + 16×4096 = 131K parameters
  Reduction:          99.2% fewer trainable parameters!

  During fine-tuning: only A and B are trained
  B is initialized to 0 → at the start: BA = 0 → no effect

hyperparams.desc: Determines the "capacity" of the LoRA adapters. Higher rank = more trainable parameters = more expressiveness, but more memory and overfitting risk.
hyperparams.recommendation: r=8 for simple tasks/little data. r=16 as a standard. r=32–64 for complex tasks with lots of data.

hyperparams.values: Typical: equal to r or 2×r (e.g. 16 or 32)
hyperparams.desc: Scaling factor for the LoRA update: actual update = (α/r) × B·A·x. Alpha/r determines the "learning strength" of the adapters.
hyperparams.recommendation: α = r (update strength = 1) or α = 2r (stronger updates). Start with α=16 (for r=16).
hyperparams.memory: No memory effect (just a scaling constant)

hyperparams.desc: Dropout within the LoRA adapter matrices. Prevents adapter overfitting, especially on small datasets.
hyperparams.recommendation: 0.05 as standard. Below 500 examples: 0.1. Above 5,000 examples: 0.0

hyperparams.values: q_proj, k_proj, v_proj, o_proj, gate_proj, up_proj, down_proj
