hyperparams.desc: Which layers of the transformer are extended with LoRA. More modules = more trainable parameters = better performance, but more memory.
hyperparams.recommendation: Minimum: q_proj + v_proj. Standard: all attention layers. Maximum: all linear layers including FFN.
hyperparams.memory: Each target_module adds ~2×r parameters per layer

hyperparams.desc: Whether bias parameters are also trained. "none" = only LoRA matrices. "all" = biases too.
hyperparams.recommendation: "none" is the default and is almost always enough.

qlora.title: QLoRA (4-bit Fine-Tuning)
qlora.subtitle: Even more efficient: quantization + LoRA combined
qlora.intro: QLoRA (Dettmers et al., 2023) combines 4-bit quantization of the base model with LoRA adapters at full precision. Result: fine-tune a 7B model in ~4 GB VRAM!
qlora.howCode:
QLoRA = quantized base model + LoRA

Step 1: Load the base model in 4-bit (NF4 quantization)
  → 7B model: 28 GB (fp32) → ~4 GB (NF4)

Step 2: Add LoRA adapters in bf16/fp16
  → Adapters stay at full precision for quality

Step 3: During training:
  → Forward pass: 4-bit weights "dequantized" to bf16 on the fly
  → Gradients only for LoRA adapters (bf16)
  → Base model stays frozen and 4-bit

Total memory for 7B:
  Base model (NF4):     ~4 GB
  LoRA adapters (bf16): ~0.3 GB
  Activations:           ~1 GB
  Optimizer states:      ~0.6 GB
  Total:                 ~6 GB → an RTX 3070 is enough!
qlora.nf4Heading: NF4 quantization – the innovation behind QLoRA
qlora.nf4Text: Standard int4 quantization quantizes weights uniformly. NF4 (4-bit NormalFloat) takes into account that transformer weights are normally distributed – it assigns bit values to boundaries that optimally partition the normal distribution:
qlora.nf4Code:
Int4 (uniform steps):
  Bit values: -8, -7, -6, ..., 7, 8  (16 levels)
  Problem: most weights lie near 0,
           extreme values are rare → poor resolution

NF4 (normally distributed steps):
  Bit values: -1, -0.69, -0.52, ..., 0.52, 0.69, 1
  (quantiles of the normal distribution)
  Advantage: better resolution where most weights lie
  Result: noticeably better quality than int4
qlora.comparisonHeading: QLoRA vs. LoRA vs. Full FT compared
qlora.footnote: GC = gradient checkpointing enabled. Quality relative to full FT fp32.
qlora.successText: Before QLoRA, 7B fine-tuning needed 28 GB+ VRAM (high-end servers only). With QLoRA it works on an RTX 3070 (8 GB VRAM). 13B models are possible with 16 GB. This makes LLM fine-tuning accessible to everyone.

peftMethoden.title: PEFT Methods Overview
peftMethoden.subtitle: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning compared
peftMethoden.intro: PEFT (Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning) is the umbrella term for methods that train only a fraction of the model parameters. LoRA is the most popular, but there are others.

methods.name: LoRA ⭐ (Low-Rank Adaptation)
methods.desc: Trains low-rank matrices alongside frozen attention weights. Standard for almost all LLM fine-tuning jobs since 2023.

methods.name: QLoRA (Quantized LoRA)
methods.desc: 4-bit quantized base model + LoRA adapters in bf16. Minimal VRAM for maximum model size.
methods.when: When VRAM is very limited (< 12 GB for 7B)

methods.name: Prefix Tuning
methods.desc: Inserts trainable "prefix" tokens at the start of every transformer layer. No modification of model weights themselves.
methods.when: When model weights must not be changed

methods.name: Prompt Tuning
methods.desc: Only "soft prompts" (trainable input embeddings) are learned. Very efficient, but weaker performance on smaller models.
methods.when: Only for very large models (> 10B) or when speed is the top priority

methods.name: Adapter Layers
methods.desc: Small feed-forward networks inserted between transformer layers. Older method (pre-LoRA), still solid.
methods.when: Historically important, mostly surpassed by LoRA today

methods.name: IA³ (Infused Adapter by Inhibiting and Amplifying)
methods.desc: Trains only scaling vectors for keys, values, and feed-forward. Extremely few parameters with good quality.
methods.when: Very little data, extremely fast iteration desired

wannWas.title: Which Fine-Tuning Method When?
wannWas.subtitle: The complete decision tree
wannWas.intro: The choice of fine-tuning method depends on VRAM, amount of data, quality requirements, and training speed. This decision tree leads you to the optimal choice.

tree.question: How much GPU VRAM do you have?

options.label: < 6 GB
options.detail: The only realistic option. RTX 3060 6GB: 7B with QLoRA is just barely possible.

options.label: 6–10 GB
options.detail: RTX 3070/4060 Ti: QLoRA for 7B, LoRA for 1B–3B models.

options.label: 12–16 GB
options.detail: RTX 3080/4070: standard LoRA very comfortable. QLoRA for 13B.

options.label: 24+ GB
options.detail: RTX 3090/4090: full FT for small models, LoRA for 7B very efficient.

tree.question: How many training examples do you have?

options.label: < 200 examples
options.detail: Training fewer parameters means less overfitting risk.

options.label: > 2,000 examples
options.result: → LoRA rank=16–32 or full FT (if VRAM allows)
options.detail: Enough data for more model capacity.

options.label: Adjust style/tone
options.detail: Simple task – little LoRA capacity needed.

options.label: Learn a new domain
options.detail: More capacity for new domain knowledge.

options.label: Fundamentally retrain
options.detail: When the model needs to change its core behavior.

options.label: Multiple tasks, one model
options.detail: Base model once, separate adapter for each task.

wannWas.recommendationText: LoRA (rank=16) + QLoRA when VRAM is tight + early stopping + cosine LR decay + weight_decay=0.01 + warmup=5% of steps. This covers nearly every fine-tuning project.

datenQualitaet.title: Data Quality & Quantity
datenQualitaet.subtitle: The most important foundation for any successful training
datenQualitaet.warningTitle: Garbage in, garbage out – the iron law of ML
datenQualitaet.warningText: The best model and the most perfect hyperparameters can't produce good results from bad data. Data quality almost always beats model size and hyperparameter tuning.
datenQualitaet.dataAmountHeading: How much data do I need?
datenQualitaet.dataAmountIntro: The amount of data needed depends heavily on the task, the base model, and the desired quality. As rough guidelines (for fine-tuning on pretrained models):
datenQualitaet.dataAmountNote: Note: These figures apply to fine-tuning on strong pretrained models. Training from scratch needs 10–100× more data.
datenQualitaet.qualityText: For LLMs, quality matters far more than quantity. 100 perfectly curated examples often beat 1,000 poor ones. The InstructGPT paper showed that 13,000 carefully selected RLHF examples were enough to turn GPT-3 into a helpful assistant.

checklist.how: Exact-match or fuzzy dedup (MinHash). Duplicates make the model weight those examples more heavily.
Manually check 5–10% of the dataset. Faulty labels are more common than expected.
Uniform encoding (UTF-8), consistent delimiters, consistent label spelling.
Filter: min_length=10 characters, max_length=2048 tokens. Remove HTML tags.
Measure the imbalance ratio. If >5:1: consider balancing (chapter 4 in this section).
Test data should match real later usage. No "easier" dataset than in production.
Strict train/val/test separation. No test material in training.
No PII (personal data) without anonymization. Check licenses of source data.

preprocessing.title: Preprocessing
preprocessing.subtitle: Preparing data optimally for maximum training quality
preprocessing.intro: Good preprocessing can improve model performance by 10–30%. It's the most underestimated phase of the ML pipeline.
preprocessing.pipelineCode:
# Complete text preprocessing pipeline:

import re
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup

def preprocess_text(text: str) -> str | None:
    # 1. Normalize encoding
    text = text.encode('utf-8', errors='ignore').decode('utf-8')
    
    # 2. Remove HTML tags
    text = BeautifulSoup(text, 'html.parser').get_text()
    
    # 3. Remove URLs (optional)
    text = re.sub(r'https?://\S+', '[URL]', text)
    
    # 4. Normalize whitespace
    text = ' '.join(text.split())
    
    # 5. Clean special characters (carefully!)
    # Careful: don't be too aggressive – punctuation matters!
    text = re.sub(r'[^\w\s\.,!?;:\'\"()\-–—€$%@#]', '', text)
    
    # 6. Filter by length
    word_count = len(text.split())
    if word_count < 5:
        return None  # Too short
    if word_count > 512:
        text = ' '.join(text.split()[:512])  # Truncate
    
    return text.strip()

# Normalize labels
def normalize_label(label: str) -> str:
    return label.strip().lower().replace(' ', '_')
preprocessing.dedupText: Duplicates in training make the model weight those examples disproportionately. With web scraping, up to 30% of a dataset can be duplicated!
preprocessing.dedupCode:
# Exact deduplication:
import hashlib

def deduplicate_exact(dataset: list[dict]) -> list[dict]:
    seen = set()
    unique = []
    for item in dataset:
        h = hashlib.md5(item['text'].encode()).hexdigest()
        if h not in seen:
            seen.add(h)
            unique.append(item)
    return unique

# Fuzzy deduplication (similar texts):
# Use MinHash / LSH for efficient near-duplicate detection
# Library: datasketch
from datasketch import MinHash, MinHashLSH
# → also finds duplicates with small variations
preprocessing.tokenText: Tokenization converts text into numbers (token IDs). Every model has its own tokenizer – the wrong tokenization means garbage data.
preprocessing.tokenCode:
# Tokenizing with the correct tokenizer:
from transformers import AutoTokenizer

# IMPORTANT: always use the base model's tokenizer!
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-hf")

# Measure token length BEFORE training:
lengths = [len(tokenizer(text)['input_ids']) for text in texts]
print(f"Median: {sorted(lengths)[len(lengths)//2]} tokens")
print(f"P95:    {sorted(lengths)[int(len(lengths)*0.95)]} tokens")
print(f"Max:    {max(lengths)} tokens")

# Choose max_length based on P95 (not max!)
# → saves memory, only loses 5% of the length
preprocessing.vramText: Attention is O(n²) with respect to sequence length. Doubling the sequence length means 4× more VRAM for attention. Choose max_length based on the 95th percentile of your data, not the maximum.

augmentation.title: Data Augmentation
augmentation.subtitle: Artificially growing and diversifying a dataset
augmentation.intro: Data augmentation creates new, meaningful training examples from existing ones. Especially valuable when data is scarce or the distribution needs to be diversified.

techniques.name: Back-translation ⭐
techniques.desc: Translate text into another language, then translate it back. Creates semantically identical, linguistically diverse variants.
techniques.example: "This product is very high-quality." → DE: "Das Produkt ist sehr hochwertig." → EN: "The product is extremely high-quality."
techniques.code:
# With Helsinki-NLP models:
from transformers import pipeline
en_to_de = pipeline("translation_en_to_de")
de_to_en = pipeline("translation_de_to_en")

def back_translate(text):
    de = en_to_de(text)[0]['translation_text']
    return de_to_en(de)[0]['translation_text']

techniques.name: LLM-based paraphrasing ⭐
techniques.desc: An LLM generates semantically equivalent variants of a text. Currently the best method for NLP augmentation.
techniques.example: "Generate 3 different phrasings with the same meaning: The device is broken."
techniques.code:
# With a local LLM (Ollama/LM Studio):
prompt = """Create 3 different phrasings 
with the exact same meaning:
{original_text}
Output only the 3 variants, one per line."""
techniques.cons: API costs or a local GPU needed

techniques.name: Synonym replacement
techniques.desc: Replace words with synonyms (via WordNet, GermaNet for German). Simple, but meaning can shift slightly.
techniques.example: "The product is good." → "The product is great."
techniques.code:
# With NLTK WordNet:
from nltk.corpus import wordnet

def replace_synonyms(text, n_replace=2):
    words = text.split()
    # Replace random words with synonyms
    ...

techniques.name: Random deletion / insertion
techniques.desc: Randomly delete, add, or swap words. Makes the model more robust to noise.
techniques.example: "The product is very good." → "The product good." (deletion)
techniques.cons: Can make texts nonsensical, low quality

augmentation.strategyCode:
Recommended augmentation pipeline:

  1. Clean the original data (preprocessing)
  2. Back-translation for important/rare classes (×2)
  3. LLM paraphrasing for critical examples (×2–3)
  4. Synonym replacement for quick multiplication (×1)

  Result: dataset size ×5–8 at high quality

Important: ALWAYS label augmented data separately
  → e.g. source: "original" vs. source: "augmented"
  → enables quality analysis after training
augmentation.warningText: Augmentation doesn't replace real data – it complements it. If possible, prioritize collecting real data. Use augmentation for rare classes or to diversify, not as a substitute.

balancing.title: Class Balancing
balancing.subtitle: Correctly handling unbalanced datasets
balancing.intro: An unbalanced dataset – e.g. 95% negative, 5% positive examples – causes the model to favor the common class. It learns to say "almost always negative": trivial, but useless.
balancing.detectCode:
# Measuring imbalance:
import pandas as pd
df = pd.read_csv('train.csv')
counts = df['label'].value_counts()
print(counts)
# negative   4750
# positive    250
# → ratio 19:1 → strong imbalance!

# Rule of thumb:
# < 3:1    → usually fine, no balancing needed
# 3:1–10:1 → balancing recommended
# > 10:1   → balancing urgently needed

# Imbalance ratio:
ratio = counts.max() / counts.min()
print(f"Imbalance ratio: {ratio:.1f}:1")

strategies.name: Class weights ⭐ (recommended)
strategies.desc: Rare classes are weighted more heavily during training. No data modification needed. The simplest and cleanest method.
strategies.code:
# Computing class weights:
from sklearn.utils.class_weight import compute_class_weight
import numpy as np

weights = compute_class_weight(
    'balanced',
    classes=np.unique(labels),
    y=labels
)
class_weights = dict(enumerate(weights))
# {0: 0.53, 1: 10.0}  ← rare class weighted 19× more

# In FrameTrain: enable "Class Weighting"
strategies.pros: No data distortion, simple, applied directly in the optimizer

strategies.name: Oversampling (multiplying the minority class)
strategies.desc: The rare class is used multiple times during training (with or without augmentation). Simple to implement.
strategies.code:
# Random oversampling:
from imblearn.over_sampling import RandomOverSampler

ros = RandomOverSampler(random_state=42)
X_res, y_res = ros.fit_resample(X, y)

# SMOTE (synthetic examples):
from imblearn.over_sampling import SMOTE
smote = SMOTE(random_state=42)
X_res, y_res = smote.fit_resample(X, y)
# SMOTE for text: back-translation is better!
strategies.pros: More training data, no information loss
strategies.cons: With random oversampling: exact duplicates → mild overfitting

strategies.name: Undersampling (reducing the majority class)
strategies.desc: The common class is reduced to the size of the rare class. Simple, but loses information.
strategies.code:
# Random undersampling:
from imblearn.under_sampling import RandomUnderSampler

rus = RandomUnderSampler(random_state=42)
X_res, y_res = rus.fit_resample(X, y)

# More controlled: stratified
min_class_size = y.value_counts().min()
balanced = df.groupby('label').sample(
    min_class_size, random_state=42)
strategies.cons: Loses data. Problematic on small datasets

strategies.name: Combination: oversample + undersample
strategies.desc: Hybrid approach: minority class boosted slightly, majority class reduced slightly. Often the best solution.
strategies.code:
# Goal: 3:1 ratio instead of 19:1
n_minority = len(df[df.label=='positive'])  # 250
target_majority = n_minority * 3  # 750

majority_down = df[df.label=='negative'].sample(target_majority)
minority_up = df[df.label=='positive'].sample(
    n_minority * 3, replace=True)  # ×3 oversample

balanced = pd.concat([majority_down, minority_up])
strategies.pros: Good balance between information loss and overfitting

balancing.metricsIntro: On unbalanced datasets, accuracy is misleading. Use instead:

metricsTable.note: Averages F1 across all classes without weighting
Best single number under extreme imbalance

balancing.recommendationText: Use class weights as the first approach – it's the simplest and usually sufficient. If the imbalance ratio is > 20:1 and class weights aren't enough: additionally oversample the minority class with data augmentation (back-translation).

mixedPrecision.title: Mixed Precision Training
mixedPrecision.subtitle: Train faster with fp16 and bf16 – half the memory
mixedPrecision.intro: Mixed precision training uses 16-bit instead of 32-bit floating point numbers for most computations. This halves memory requirements and speeds up training on modern GPUs by 2–3×.
mixedPrecision.comparisonHeading: fp32 vs. fp16 vs. bf16 – the detailed comparison
mixedPrecision.bf16Heading: Why bf16 is better than fp16
mixedPrecision.bf16Text: BFloat16 has the same exponent range as fp32 (8 exponent bits), but fewer mantissa bits. fp16 has only 5 exponent bits → much smaller numeric range → overflow with large activations. bf16 solves this problem.
mixedPrecision.bf16Code:
fp16 overflow problem:
  If activations > 65,504:
  → Overflow → NaN → training crashes!
  Workaround: loss scaling (automatic, but complex)

bf16 no overflow:
  Same numeric range as fp32
  → No loss scaling needed
  → Simpler, more stable

GPU support for bf16:
  NVIDIA Ampere (RTX 30xx, A100): bf16 hardware support ✓
  NVIDIA Turing (RTX 20xx, T4):   fp16 hardware only ✗
  NVIDIA Volta (V100):             fp16 hardware only ✗
  Apple Silicon (M1/M2/M3):       bf16 hardware support ✓
  AMD RDNA3+:                      bf16 hardware support ✓
mixedPrecision.ampText: During mixed precision training, not all computations run in 16-bit. Some critical operations stay in fp32:
mixedPrecision.ampCode:
Mixed precision training – what runs at which precision:

  fp16/bf16 (fast, low memory):
    ✓ Forward pass (activations)
    ✓ Backward pass (gradients)
    ✓ Model weights (inference copy)

  fp32 (safe, precise):
    ✓ Master copy of weights (optimizer states)
    ✓ Weight updates
    ✓ Loss computation
    ✓ Batch normalization (statistics)

  Result: ~2× less VRAM, 2–3× faster
          with minimal quality loss
mixedPrecision.frameTrainText: FrameTrain automatically picks the best precision format based on your GPU. RTX 30xx/40xx and newer: automatically bf16. Older GPUs: fp16 or fp32. You can override this manually in the "Advanced Settings" panel.

gradientCheckpointing.title: Gradient Checkpointing
gradientCheckpointing.subtitle: Halve VRAM by selectively recomputing activations
gradientCheckpointing.intro: Gradient checkpointing (also called activation checkpointing) is a technique that reduces VRAM requirements at the cost of some compute time. Ideal when VRAM is the limiting factor.
gradientCheckpointing.problemText: During normal backpropagation, all intermediate activations (outputs of every layer) must be stored in VRAM to compute gradients:
gradientCheckpointing.problemCode:
Normal training (N=32 layers, L=7B):
  Forward pass: store all 32 layer outputs
  Activation memory: ~4–8 GB (at BS=4, seq=512)
  
  Problem: with large BS or long sequences:
    BS=16, seq=2048 → ~32 GB for activations ALONE!

Gradient checkpointing:
  Store only every Kth "checkpoint" (e.g. every 4th layer)
  During the backward pass: unstored activations
                            are RECOMPUTED
  
  Trade-off:
    Memory: ÷2 to ÷4  (roughly 30–60% less)
    Speed: ×0.7–0.8 (20–30% slower)
gradientCheckpointing.savingsHeading: How much memory gradient checkpointing saves
gradientCheckpointing.comboCode:
Maximum memory savings – all tricks combined:

  Base 7B model (fp32):                    28 GB VRAM
  
  + Mixed precision (bf16):          ÷2    14 GB
  + LoRA (train only adapters):      ÷3    ~5 GB gradients
  + QLoRA (4-bit base):              ÷4    ~3.5 GB base
  + Gradient checkpointing:          -2GB  ~4 GB activations
  + Gradient accumulation (BS=1):    -1GB  ~3 GB batches
  
  Total:  ~5–6 GB VRAM for 7B fine-tuning!
          → Possible on an RTX 3070 (8 GB) ✓

  Without any optimization: 60+ GB → impossible on a consumer GPU
gradientCheckpointing.frameTrainText: In FrameTrain, you can enable gradient checkpointing in the Training Panel. It's automatically recommended when the system detects VRAM might get tight. Rule of thumb: always enable it when VRAM < 12 GB and the model has > 1B parameters.
gradientCheckpointing.whenText: If training speed matters and VRAM is sufficient: leave it disabled. If VRAM is the bottleneck or OOM errors occur: enable it. The 20–30% speed penalty is usually acceptable to make training possible at all.

earlyStopping.title: Early Stopping
earlyStopping.subtitle: Automatically stop at the optimal point
earlyStopping.intro: Early stopping monitors the validation loss and automatically stops training when no meaningful improvement is happening anymore. It automatically loads the best checkpoint.
earlyStopping.whyHeading: Why early stopping matters so much
withoutItems: The model overfits past the optimal point
Wasteful: training past the overfitting point is useless
withItems: Training stops automatically at the optimal point
The best checkpoint is loaded automatically
No overfitting from training too long
Saves compute time when a plateau is reached early

params.desc: How many epochs without improvement are tolerated before stopping.
params.effect: Too low: stops too early (during temporary fluctuations). Too high: overfitting happens anyway.
params.recommendation: patience=3 for short training runs. patience=5 for long training runs with fluctuations.

params.desc: Minimum improvement in validation loss to count as "better".
params.effect: Prevents stopping when only minimal noise slightly improves the metric.
params.recommendation: min_delta=0.001 is a good starting value.

params.effect: val_loss is more stable and direct. val_accuracy can plateau while val_loss is still decreasing.

params.desc: Whether the best checkpoint is automatically loaded when stopping.
params.effect: Without: last checkpoint (after overfitting). With: best checkpoint loaded.
params.recommendation: Always True. Only exception: if the last checkpoint is explicitly desired.

earlyStopping.strategyHeading: Early stopping strategy by training type
earlyStopping.strategyCode:
Short fine-tuning (3–5 planned epochs):
  patience = 2
  min_delta = 0.001
  → Little tolerance for fluctuations

Medium fine-tuning (5–20 epochs):
  patience = 3
  min_delta = 0.0005
  → Standard setup

Long fine-tuning (20+ epochs):
  patience = 5
  min_delta = 0.0001
  → Enough tolerance for learning curve plateaus

Combined with an LR scheduler (ReduceLROnPlateau):
  LR is reduced on plateau, then 3 more epochs are tried
  → Early stopping only after that → better use of training
bestPractices: ALWAYS monitor val_loss, not train_loss (train_loss keeps decreasing forever)
Enable restore_best_weights=True – otherwise you load the worst model
A patience of 3 survives short spikes without being too lenient
Combine with cosine LR decay: LR cools down before early stopping kicks in
With little data: base early stopping on validation F1, not just loss

ensembles.title: Model Ensembles
ensembles.subtitle: Combine multiple models for maximum performance
ensembles.intro: Ensembles combine the predictions of multiple models. They're almost always better than a single model – at the cost of more compute at inference time.

methods.name: Majority Voting (Classification)
methods.desc: Each model gives one class prediction. The most common class wins (like an election).
methods.code:
# Combining 3 model predictions:
predictions = [model1.predict(x),  # "positive"
               model2.predict(x),  # "positive"  
               model3.predict(x)]  # "negative"
from collections import Counter
final = Counter(predictions).most_common(1)[0][0]
# → "positive" (2 of 3 models)
methods.use: When class labels (not probabilities) are available

methods.name: Probability Averaging ⭐ (Recommended)
methods.desc: Softmax probabilities of all models are averaged. Then the highest probability determines the class.
methods.code:
# Averaging probabilities:
probs_1 = model1.predict_proba(x)  # [0.8, 0.2]
probs_2 = model2.predict_proba(x)  # [0.6, 0.4]
probs_3 = model3.predict_proba(x)  # [0.9, 0.1]

avg_probs = np.mean([probs_1, probs_2, probs_3], axis=0)
# → [0.77, 0.23]
final_class = np.argmax(avg_probs)  # class 0 = "positive"
methods.use: When softmax probabilities are available – almost always better than majority voting

methods.name: Checkpoint Ensemble
methods.desc: Different checkpoints from the same training run are combined. Cost-effective – no separate training needed.
methods.code:
# Last N checkpoints from one training run:
checkpoints = [
    'model_epoch_8.pt',
    'model_epoch_9.pt', 
    'model_epoch_10.pt'
]
# Average predictions from all checkpoints
ensemble_probs = np.mean([
    load_and_predict(ckpt, x) 
    for ckpt in checkpoints
], axis=0)
methods.use: When multiple full training runs are too expensive – reuses existing checkpoints

methods.name: Diverse Ensemble (different seeds/HPs)
methods.desc: Same model, but different random seeds or slightly different hyperparameters. More diversity = better.
methods.code:
# 5 runs with different seeds:
models = []
for seed in [42, 123, 456, 789, 1337]:
    model = train_with_seed(seed, lr=2e-5)
    models.append(model)

# Ensemble of the 5 models:
ensemble_pred = np.mean([
    m.predict_proba(x) for m in models
], axis=0)
methods.use: Competitions, when maximum performance is needed

methods.name: Stacking (Meta-Learning)
methods.desc: A meta-model learns to optimally combine the outputs of the base models. More complex but more powerful.
methods.code:
# Stacking procedure:
# 1. Train base models (cross-validation)
# 2. Use base model predictions as features
# 3. Train a meta-model on these features

# Simplest meta-learner: logistic regression
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
meta_features = np.column_stack([
    model1.predict_proba(X_val),
    model2.predict_proba(X_val),
    model3.predict_proba(X_val)
])
meta_model = LogisticRegression().fit(meta_features, y_val)
methods.use: When base models are very different (different architectures)

ensembles.diversityHeading: Ensemble diversity – the key to success

whatIs.title: What is FrameTrain?
whatIs.paragraph1: FrameTrain is a desktop app for local machine learning training that lets data scientists, ML engineers, and researchers train AI models directly on their own hardware – with no cloud dependency.
whatIs.paragraph2: With native HuggingFace integration, you get access to thousands of pretrained models that you can efficiently adapt to your own datasets via LoRA fine-tuning. Whether NLP, computer vision, or LLM fine-tuning – FrameTrain supports common PyTorch and Transformers-based workflows.
whatIs.paragraph3: Unlike cloud solutions such as AWS SageMaker, Google Colab, or Paperspace, your data and models never leave your device. That makes FrameTrain the ideal solution for GDPR-compliant ML projects in companies and research environments.
whatIs.paragraph4: GPU acceleration (NVIDIA CUDA, Apple Metal), automatic model checkpointing, and live training-metric monitoring are built in by default.

faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.subtitle: Everything you need to know about FrameTrain

items.question: Can I fine-tune LLMs with FrameTrain?
items.answer: Yes. FrameTrain supports fine-tuning of Large Language Models (LLMs) via LoRA and QLoRA. You can import models like Llama, Mistral, or Phi directly from HuggingFace and adapt them to your own datasets.

items.question: Which GPUs are supported?
items.answer: FrameTrain runs on all NVIDIA GPUs with CUDA support (from the GTX 10 series onward) as well as on Apple Silicon (M1/M2/M3/M4) via Metal Performance Shaders. Pure CPU-based training is also possible.

items.question: What's the difference compared to Google Colab or AWS SageMaker?
items.answer: FrameTrain runs entirely locally on your machine. There are no ongoing cloud costs, your data is never uploaded, and you're not dependent on an internet connection. Ideal for sensitive or proprietary datasets.

items.question: Do I need programming knowledge?
items.answer: No. FrameTrain is designed as a graphical application. You configure training parameters through an intuitive interface without writing any code. Advanced users can also use configuration files.

items.question: Does FrameTrain support LoRA training?
items.answer: Yes, LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) is fully integrated. You can configure LoRA rank, alpha, and target modules directly in the app, which also enables fine-tuning on GPUs with limited VRAM.

items.question: Why does FrameTrain only cost €4.99/month?
items.answer: FrameTrain is in Early Access. The price is intentionally low so that as many developers and researchers as possible can get access early. The price increases to €9.99 after the first 100 users – sign up now to lock in the lower price permanently.

items.question: Does FrameTrain run on Apple M1/M2/M3/M4?
items.answer: Yes. FrameTrain natively supports Apple Silicon via Metal Performance Shaders (MPS). Models are accelerated on the M-chip's GPU without requiring CUDA.

items.question: Which model formats are supported?
items.answer: FrameTrain supports PyTorch models (.pt, .bin), SafeTensors, and the GGUF format. Models can be imported directly from the HuggingFace Hub or loaded locally.

items.question: Can I use my own datasets?
items.answer: Yes. You can load your own datasets in CSV, JSON, or JSONL format. FrameTrain also supports datasets directly from the HuggingFace Datasets Hub.

items.question: Does FrameTrain support mixed precision and gradient checkpointing?
items.answer: Yes. FP16 and BF16 mixed-precision training are fully supported, as is gradient checkpointing for memory-efficient training on GPUs with limited VRAM.
